name: The Law of Specifics.
rule: In cases where rules conflict with each other, specific rules always take precedence over general rules. Effects on game elements should be assumed to have a greater weight than those in any rulebook.

name: The Law of Cruel Fates.
rule: In cases where you cannot determine how to follow a rule, select the one that is the least beneficial/most detrimental to the players.

name: Game Element.
rule:
A game element is any physical component of the game, a story passage, or a note the game instructed you to take.

1. **Generic Tokens.** Use Generic tokens to keep track of anything that you want to; these were added for your convenience. Whenever there is an effect that does not use any of the provided specific tokens, you will be instructed to use a generic token to track it.

name: Random.
rule:
When you are asked to choose a random element, do the following based on the element:

1. **Random Card.** Draw the top card from the indicated (facedown) deck.

   2. **Random Direction.** Draw the top card from the Minor Trauma deck. The arrow in the bottom left corner designates the randomly chosen direction. If it shows a rotating arrow, you may instead choose a direction. Place the drawn card at the bottom of the deck.

3. **(other) Random element.** Roll one d10 for each element, lowest roll is selected.

name: Ignore.
rule:
When one or more entities “Ignore” a game element or rule, do not resolve that game element or rule for those entities.

1. **Disable.** “Disabled” elements or rules are Ignored by all entities until stated, or until the end of any current Battle if not.

2. **Immune**[12](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.1ne4njd6mpm4)**.** If an entity is “immune” to a game element or rule, it Ignores the effects of that game element or rule but does not prevent its resolution (it may still affect other entities).

name: Remove.
rule:
“Removed” elements are set aside and not used until instructed, or until the end of Battle if not.

1. **Discard.** Treat “discard” as “remove”, except the discarded pieces are placed either in their respective discard section/pile, if one exists, or back into their collective pool if not, and are still considered in-play.

   1. **Discarding Trauma Cards.** Discarded trauma cards are put on the bottom of their respective deck.

2. **Archiving (Permanently Removing).** When instructed to Permanently Remove a game element (or Archive it), place it back in the game box in its original place. It is no longer considered a part of the game state ([I.2.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.8ayznd73asdm)), though an effect may instruct you to return it.

name: Exhaust.
rule:
When instructed to exhaust a game element or ability box, turn it 90 degrees or place a Generic Token on it to denote this. Game elements remain exhausted until they are unexhausted by another effect.

1. **Unexhaust.** When instructed to unexhaust a game element, turn it back to right-side-up if rotated or remove a generic token on it that was used to mark it as exhausted.

2. **Exhausting Non-Cards.** Some game elements other than cards may instruct you to exhaust them. In that case, you only exhaust the specific related part of that game element.

name: Cannot.
rule: The term “cannot” is absolute. It cannot be overridden unless explicitly instructed.

name: Gain.
rule:
Whenever you gain a game element, simply find it and add it to an appropriate place:

   1. **Gaining Gear.** Gained Gear cards are added to the Argo Armory deck.

   1. **Gaining Gear in Battle.** If you gain a Gear card during a Battle, the Titan responsible for the gain may immediately equip that card. If the gained card has the Possession keyword, the Titan must immediately equip it. You must still abide by slot limits. If equipping is optional, and you choose not to equip a gained Gear card, it is added to the Armory instead.

2. **Gaining Tech.** Gained Technology cards are added to the Technology deck.

3. **Gaining Clues.** Gained Clue and Ally cards are placed next to your current Story and Doom cards.

4. **Gaining Mnemos cards.** Gained Mnemos cards are placed in the play area of the Argonaut who gained them. If it is impossible for any Argonaut to gain a Mnemos card ([III.5.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.a30kz9to37f2)), instead add it to the Mnemos Vault ([III.5.6](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.knkix1rxv7ou)).

   5. **Gaining Tokens.** Gained tokens are placed in the appropriate area.

   1. **Battle Tokens.** Tokens that have no effect outside of Battle (e.g. Ambrosia or Despair) are held by the Argonaut who gained them.

6. **Gaining Other Game Elements.** Some game elements are not represented by physical components. If you gain any element that is not represented by a component, note it on either the Argo Sheet or an Argonaut Sheet, whichever is specified.

7. **Loss.** When you lose a physical component, archive it unless instructed otherwise. When you lose a game element that is not represented by a physical component, erase it from the appropriate sheet (if it is singular) or lower its value by a specified number.

8. **Gain/Loss Mitigation.** Some Technology abilities may allow you to ignore gaining or losing a specific game element ([VIII.6.4.2d](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.wrosahkjqeb0)).

9. **Voluntary Gain/Loss.** If a gain/loss is listed in the text of an active ability, story choice ([IX.1.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.y1kqcktjb9r8)), or any other non-forced effect, it is considered voluntary (a “payment”).

   1. **Unmitigable.** Voluntary gain/loss cannot be mitigated or ignored in any way.

10. **Gaining/Losing as a Group.** When you are instructed to “gain as a group” or “lose as a group", distribute the specified statistic or element among all of the Argonauts as you see fit; it does not have to be distributed evenly.

name: Distance.
rule: A number of spaces or tiles. Distance between spaces or tiles is always measured orthogonally, never diagonally.

name: Game State.
rule: The "game state" refers to the collective set of conditions, variables, and rules that define the current situation and progress of the game being played. It encompasses all relevant information regarding the positions, statuses, resources, actions the players can take, and game elements within the game environment.

name: Effect.
rule:
An 'effect' is any text found on a game element, including the rulebook, that has the potential to alter the game state upon activation or meeting certain conditions. This includes symbols used as abbreviations for text. Effects are separated by periods ('.').

1. **Effect Clarifications.**

   1. A game element is never an effect, nor vice versa. A game element can *have* effects, and an effect can *modify* a game element.

   2. Titles, sub-titles, headings, and subheadings are never effects, but may be modifiers. These serve as identifiers or labels for game elements or abilities and do not directly alter the game state.

   3. Text that is in quotes or is italicized is only part of an effect if it is simply the name of another element, otherwise it is never an effect.

   4. The number+letter code in the bottom left of a card is simply the card identifier, which is not an effect.

   5. Text and symbols on AI, BP, gear, map, Mnemos, Fated Mnemos, Trait, Godform, Nymph, Exploration, Terrain, Single/Double Wound, Moiros, Kratos, Pattern, Trauma, and Condition cards and any cards you find in secret decks or envelopes, as well as Titan, Primordial, Doom, Progress, and Inward Odyssey sheets typically represent game mechanics, abilities, or conditions that directly impact the game state and are thus considered effects.

   6. All other text and symbols on Technology cards are effects, except the “requirements”, which serve as conditions for gaining the Technology card rather than directly altering the game state.

   7. Clue cards' text qualifies as an effect only if it instructs a change in the game state. Otherwise, it serves as narrative or thematic information.

   8. Any code that leads you to a paragraph in a storybook is an effect.

   9. Tokens are not effects.

   10. Traits on cards are never effects, but are modifiers.

name: Types of Effects.
rule:
1. **Active Effects.** An Active Effect is an effect that costs something to use. Active effects end once Resolved.

2. **Constant Effects.** A Constant Effect is an effect with no cost to use. Constant effects must be resolved every time their timing condition is met and end once Resolved.

name: Resolving Effects.
rule:
To Resolve an effect, follow its instruction through to completion. This may require you to find those instructions in this rulebook (search for it in the Master Index). Afterwards, the effect is considered Resolved.

1. **Effect Ordering.** Always resolve effects in the order you read them in (left to right, top to bottom).

2. **Completely Resolve.** Always completely resolve one effect (and any effects that it triggers) before moving on to the next listed effect on a game element.

name: Triggers.
rule:
A “Trigger” is a condition that an effect or modifier can have. An effect with a trigger does not resolve unless its trigger’s condition is met. When a trigger’s condition is met, its linked effect must be resolved immediately, before resolving any effects other than the ones caused or enabled by it. Triggers’ linked effects resolve before their cause effect finishes resolving[7](#bookmark=kix.ncneqmlyd9ka).

1. **Not Effects.** Triggers are never effects and cannot resolve.

2. **Activating Effects.** To “Activate” an effect is synonymous with “triggering” that effect. An effect has been “activated” when it has been triggered. If an effect is optional, it only is considered activated if the players chose to resolve it.

name: Modifiers (Passives).
rule:
A “modifier” (also called a Passive) is any text on any game element that applies to an effect and changes how it resolves. A modifier can be permanent or temporary.

1. **Not Effects.** Modifiers are never effects and cannot resolve.

name: Immediately.
rule: If an effect contains the word “immediately” it must be resolved the moment it is triggered, takes precedence over any other lingering effects, overruling any conflicting rules, and ignoring any listed timing requirements on defined effects.

name: Instead.
rule: The word “Instead” is a modifier that replaces an effect with another effect. The original effect is Ignored. *(For example, “Opening 2 instead” on a Gear card means that the value of its original “Opening X” keyword is Ignored in favor of the new effect.)*

name: May and Must.
rule:
1. **May.** When instructed to resolve an effect with the word “may”, you must choose whether or not to resolve the effect. Regardless of your choice, the effect is considered resolved.

2. **Must.** If an effect includes the word “must”, you have no choice but to resolve it.

3. **Without “May”.** If an effect does not include the word “may”, you must resolve it.

name: When.
rule: When instructed to resolve an effect with the word “when”, resolve this effect after you resolve the effect it refers to (or, in some cases, when you begin to resolve it), but before any other actions or effects. *(For example, if an effect triggers “When you draw a Trauma card,” it activates immediately after you draw the card but before you resolve it.)*

name: When you are about to.
rule: When instructed to resolve an effect with the phrase “when you are about to”, resolve it immediately before you resolve the effect that is about to happen. *(i.e. if an ability is triggered “when you are about to die,” it activates after another effect instructs you to die, but before it is actually resolved.)*

name: Timing-Conflict Resolution.
rule:
If more than one effect is triggered at the same time, you may choose the order in which these effects are resolved, unless stated otherwise. If the chosen resolution order causes any of these effects to make another impossible or unviable, Ignore the impossible/ unviable one. *(Which effect is impossible/unviable could be different with different resolution orders.)*

1. **One Resolution**[9](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.63fkdh4as5zy)**.** An effect cannot be added to the timing-conflict after the resolution has started. In addition, such effects no longer meet their timing requirement because the window has passed. *(Effects with the same timing condition all happen simultaneously, and are then broken down by the Timing-Conflict Resolution.)*

name: ‘And’ Effects
rule: [7](#bookmark=kix.ncneqmlyd9ka)**.** When resolving an effect with the word ‘and’ in it, resolve any effects triggered by its clauses one at a time from left to right, and before any following effects. *(It is still a single effect because it is one sentence.)*

name: Owned Effects.
rule:
All effects in the game are “owned” by one of the following entities: an Argonaut, a Titan, the Primordial, the Argo, or the Game itself. An entity owns an effect if that effect is on any game element that is exclusive to that entity.

   1. **Granting Ownership.** Some effects grant ownership of a new effect to another entity if the effect specifies, typically as “X may do Y”, where X is the entity to whom the ownership is granted and Y is the new effect. *(For example, the Motivate X keyword gives ownership of the effect ‘Move X spaces’ to the chosen Titan. This, importantly, does not transfer the ownership of the original effect (Motivate X).)*

   2. **Ownership Clarifications.**

   1. *Argonauts own all effects of all game elements present on their respective Argonaut sheet, including the sheet itself. This includes Mnemoses, Fated Mnemoses, stats, and specific Argonaut abilities.*

   2. *A Titan owns all effects on its equipped gear cards and all abilities on its Titan sheet, as well as all effects that its Argonaut owns.*

   3. *A Primordial owns the effects on all game elements that are specific to that Primordial, such as its AI cards, BP cards, Trait cards, all effects on its Primordial sheet, etc.*

   4. *The Argo owns all effects on tech cards. This is most pertinent in Battles with Argo Abilities.*

   5. *Any effect not owned by one of the above entities is owned by the Game.*

name: Voluntary/Involuntary.
rule: Effects are voluntary if the affected entity (defined above in [I.2.13](#bookmark=id.gjrc53z0r2ft)) owns the effect. Effects are involuntary if the affected entity does not own the effect.

name: Gates.
rule:
Effects and modifiers may be Gated by certain requirements. Gates are found near either the top or left side of a different-colored box on a game element. If the Gate requirement is met, the effect or modifier within the different-colored box is treated as if it was part of the ability box or effect, otherwise it is treated as blank[14](#bookmark=kix.vncfldnua34g).
   1. **Colored Boxes.** Many game elements have different-colored boxes on them with effects and/or modifiers within them. If such a box has a Gate, the effects/modifiers within are ignored unless the Gate requirements are met.
   2. **Gate Requirements.** Gate requirements are met when the amount of the statistic or token specified by the Gate is met or surpassed by the relevant entity (usually a specific Titan). If there is an ‘OR’ listed in the Gate, only one of the two listed effects must be met. If there is an ‘&’, both of the listed effects must be met.
3. **Losing and Regaining Gates.** If the gate requirement stops being met, the effect or modifier is again locked and unavailable. If you meet it again, the effects or modifiers are again active. If the effect is a simple gain/loss, it does not retrigger[4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.sn94cfg9mzlo).

name: Ability Box.
rule: An ability box is any graphic container with effects inside on a Gear card, Titan sheet, Mnemos card, or Technology card.

name: Ability.
rule:
An ability is any effect within an Ability Box.

1. **Asterisk Text**[5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.k2ihbmncjqiu)**.** All effects and modifiers within an Ability Box that are preceded by an asterisk (\*) are not considered abilities nor as a part of the Ability Box.

name: Ability Usage.
rule:
Abilities with no timing condition can only be used as a free action during their owner’s turn, all other abilities can only be used when their timing condition is met.

1. **Active Abilities.** Each available active ([I.2.3.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.cfxmzk8vj4ev)) ability on a single game element can only be used once per Round.

name: Active Ability Costs.
rule:
If any of the following are listed before an ability, you must resolve all of them in order to activate that ability.

1. **Fate/Rage/Danger.** If any Triskelion stat is listed, you must gain the shown amount of that stat in order to activate the ability.

2. **Exhaust.** If the Exhaust symbol is listed, you must exhaust that ability box in order to activate the ability.

3. **Discard.** If the Discard symbol is listed, you must discard the game element the ability box is on in order to activate the ability.

4. **Tokens.** If a specific token symbol is listed, if it is a positive/negative token, you must discard/gain (respectively) one of that token in order to activate the ability.

5. **Actions.** If an Action icon is listed, you must spend the indicated Action in order to use that ability. This is either a Combat Action, Movement Action, or both *(the ‘both’ option is sometimes referred to as a “Complicated Action”)*.

name: Named Abilities.
rule:
The text of some abilities is preceded by an ability name and, optionally, a subtype, preceding their name. These are called Named Abilities.

1. **One At a Time.** You cannot use multiple abilities with the same name at the same time (Stackable Keywords break this rule, see [I.3.6.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.dqwxdwx561lr)).

2. **Same Round.** You can use multiple abilities with the same name during a single Round.

3. **Flavor-named Abilities.** Some abilities have a name that only functions for flavor. These are written as a bolded word or phrase followed by a colon before an ability.

name: Keywords.
rule:
A keyword is a term used as shorthand for an ability used to convey specific common rules. All keywords are named abilities. See Appendix A for the list of all keywords and their definitions.

1. **Stackable Keywords.** A Stackable Keyword is any Keyword followed by an ‘X’, unless stated otherwise in its definition. Multiple instances of a Stackable Keyword must be activated simultaneously, unless stated otherwise.

name: Timing Conditions.
rule:
Timing conditions on abilities determine when they can trigger (for constant abilities) or be activated (for active abilities).

1. **Reactions.** Abilities with the Reaction icon can only be resolved during a Window of Opportunity ([VI.6.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.maywt22hqk0b)).

2. **Named Conditions.** There are several named conditions used as shorthand for common requirements needed to trigger abilities. Named conditions look identical to Flavor-named abilities ([I.3.5.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.9b72i86ci1)), they are differentiable because they include exclusively the following:

1. **“Crit Evade:”.** Resolve the ability that follows when you roll a natural ‘10’ with the Crit Die ([I.4.1.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.h2hv0pqoghti)) during the Evasion Roll step of a Primordial Attack.

   2. **“Crit Evade Fail:”.** Resolve the ability that follows each time you roll a natural ‘1’ with the Crit Die during the Evasion Roll of a Primordial Attack.

3. **“Crit Chance:”.** Resolve the ability that follows when you gain a Crit Chance ([X.5.3.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.7yl2h2yne8xw)).

4. **“Crit Miss:”.** Resolve this ability each time you roll a natural ‘1’ with the Crit Die ([I.4.1.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.h2hv0pqoghti)) on an Attack Roll.

   5. **“Each Hit:”.** Add once instance of the ability that follows to the ability box for each hit with your Attack dice during a Titan Attack. If the ability doesn’t have a timing, it resolves immediately.

   6. **“Each Miss:”.** Add once instance of the ability that follows to the ability box for each miss with your Attack dice during a Titan Attack. If the ability doesn’t have a timing, it resolves immediately.

   7. **“Fail:”.** Resolve this ability after the Power Roll step of a Titan Attack if your Power did not surpass the AT value.

   8. **“Full Hit:”.** Resolve this ability when you hit with all of your Attack dice during a Titan Attack.

9. **“Full Miss:”.** Resolve the ability that follows when you Full Miss ([X.5.4.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.gnoz2qe90vkq)) an Attack.

   10. **“Sunbask X:”.** In order to use the ability that follows, discard X Fire tokens from the Kratos Pool.

   11. **“Sunstarved:”.** Resolve the ability that follows only if there are no Fire tokens in the Kratos Pool.

   12. **“Wound:”.** Resolve the ability that follows after the Power Roll step of a Titan Attack if your Power surpassed the AT value.

name: D10s.
rule:
d10s are used for Attack Rolls, Evasion Rolls, Tests, and other miscellaneous Rolls. Treat the Progress symbol result as a ‘10’. A natural ‘10’ (the result on the die without modifiers) on a d10 is always a success/hit whereas a natural ‘1’ is always a fail/miss. These dice are not piece limited.

1. **Crit Die.** White d10s are called Crit Dice, and one of the dice in all Attack and Evasion rolls must be a Crit Die. Crit Dice have no significance outside of Attack and Evasion rolls.

name: Power Dice.
rule: Power dice are used for Power Rolls and Armor Rolls. They come in four different types—Red, Black, White, and Mortal (gray)—and they are limited by the number of dice provided in the game box ([X.5.7.1b](#bookmark=id.hg5499hvu7q5)). Power dice consist of three different symbols: Power, Potentials, and Dots. *(There are 6 Red Power Dice, 4 Black Power Dice, and 3 White Power Dice in the Core game).*

name: Rerolling Dice.
rule:
Many effects will allow you to re-roll some of your dice. For a single effect, each die can only be re-rolled once, and all dice being re-rolled must be declared before doing any re-rolls. If multiple players are rolling for the same effect at the same time, they must all declare whether they want to re-roll their dice before any of them does so.

1. **Fate Re-rolls.** You can gain Fate to re-roll d10s (only), 1 Fate for each die you decide to re-roll. If the d10 was rolled for the Argo instead of an Argonaut, you must gain Argo Fate instead.

name: Basic Argo Stats.
rule:
1. **Crew.** Crew represents the strength of the able-bodied members of the Argo’s crew. Losing too much Crew will lead to a campaign loss.

   2. **Hull.** Hull represents the integrity of Argo’s hull. Losing too much Hull will lead to a campaign loss.

3. **Argo Fate.** Argo Fate represents the collective destiny of the Argo and its crew. Like the Triskelion stat ([III.3.3.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.pjoudbvkqtm6)), the higher your Argo Fate, the more negative repercussions you’ll face. Gaining Argo Fate over 9 will lead to a campaign loss.

4. **Argo Knowledge.** Argo Knowledge represents the collective knowledge about the Argo and the state of the world. it is a cumulative stat that will only ever go up ([IX.2.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.il7z0y1mfqfp)).

   1. **Argo Knowledge Limit.** You cannot gain more than 20 Argo Knowledge in a Cycle. *(e.g. You start Cycle 2 with 20 Argo Knowledge, so your maximum for the Cycle is 40.)*

name: Resources.
rule:
Resources are mainly gained from the Exploration deck and Primordial Battles. They are primarily used to craft Gear cards and activate certain Technology abilities. They differ between Cycles.

1. **Mortal Resources.** Mortal resources are primarily gained from Exploration cards ([VIII.3.2.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.bzmrysj4xj4y)). They are noted in the Mortal part of the Cargo Hold section on the Argo Sheet.

2. **Primordial Resources.** Primordial resources are primarily gained from Primordial Battles ([X.6.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.hk4bei7sq4a3)). They are noted in the Primordial part of the Cargo Hold section on the Argo Sheet.

   1. **Primordial Cores.** Of Primordial resources, Cores are the most valuable. You gain Cores from critically wounded BP III cards. When you gain a Core, write its Primordial’s name and update its quantity in the Cargo Hold under Cores.

   3. **Divine Resources.** Divine resources represent extremely rare items that are gained when instructed. They are noted in the Hidden Hold section of the Argo Sheet.

4. **Rare Resources.** Rare resources include all resources (other than Cores) not listed on the Argo Sheet. When you gain a Rare resource, write both its name and quantity under Rare Resources.

name: Types of Divine Resources.
rule:
1. **Priests.** Priests have no inherent effects, but some Technology abilities may have them as a cost.

2. **Sisyphus Tears.** Sisyphus Tears are used to replay Battles ([X.6.1.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.g3qkr9wybw6u)).

3. **Pygmalion Stones.** Pygmalion Stones are used to save titans who died in Battle ([X.6.4.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.swnh7yvy4xe6)).

1. **Gaining.** Ignore this rule until instructed otherwise. Whenever an Argo-bred Titan dies, make a Pygmalion mark. When you have 3 Pygmalion marks, gain a Pygmalion Stone and erase all marks. A Pygmalion Stone gained during the Casualties step is too late to be used on another Titan that died that same Battle[4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.sn94cfg9mzlo).

4. **Echoes of Recollection.** When an Argonaut dies, before resolving any other effects, you may spend an Echo of Recollection to ignore that Argonaut death. This cannot be used to “save” an Argonaut from a Mnemos Breakthrough ([III.5.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.9m3z4mfbg5ne)).

   1. **Gaining.** Ignore this rule until instructed otherwise. Whenever an Argonaut dies, make an Echoes of Recollection mark. When you have 3 Echoes of Recollection marks, gain an Echoes of Recollection and erase all marks.

name: Diplomacy Score.
rule:
Factions are represented by your Diplomacy Score and Diplomacy Modifier with that Faction, and many game elements will refer to those values.

   1. **Cycle-Specific.** Each Cycle has a different set of Factions.

   2. **Diplomacy Score.** Your standing with a given Faction is called a Diplomacy Score, represented by a positive or negative number.

   3. **Diplomacy Track.** The Diplomacy track on the Argo sheet is for noting your current standing with each Faction in the Cycle. Whenever you gain or lose Diplomacy with a Faction, mark the appropriate space on the corresponding track.

   1. **Diplomacy Thresholds.** Each track is divided into sections representing different Diplomacy Thresholds (like Friendly or Denounced).

   2. **Diplomacy Modifier.** The numbers in parentheses above the track sections denote the Diplomacy Modifier you should apply if you are within a given threshold. Whenever you test Diplomacy, modify your result by that value.

4. **Diplomacy Triggers.** Various game elements will include Diplomacy triggers ([I.2.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.igiqrz9gefzz)) that refer to your Diplomacy Threshold or Score with the Local Faction ([VIII.1.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.f74cwg9o0jyi)).

5. **At War.** If you ever reach the At War section of any Faction’s Diplomacy track, ignore Diplomacy gain and loss with that Faction.

name: Events.
rule: Events are anything written on the Timeline.

name: Adding Events.
rule: When instructed to add an Event to the Timeline, note its name on the indicated day.

name: Types of Events.
rule:
The type of Event determines when during a Campaign Round it is resolved.

1. **Battle Events.** Battle events trigger during the Encounter Step.

2. **Advancement Events.** Advancement events trigger during the Advancement Step.

3. **Story Events.** Story events trigger during the Story Step.

name: Progress.
rule: For each Progress you gain, you must place 1 Progress token on your current Story card or the Inward Odyssey card ([IX.2.4](#bookmark=id.il7z0y1mfqfp)), if possible.

name: Story Cards.
rule:
Story cards are how you win Aeon Trespass: Odyssey. After completing a Cycle’s story introduction, you will gain Story card 1 on side A (Story Card 1A).

1. **Requirements.** Main story progression is resolved when you fulfill all requirements noted on your current Story card. This is frequently finding a specific map tile or placing a certain number of Progress tokens on it.

2. **Timing.** When you meet the current Story card’s requirements, you will resolve its story progression during the Story Step ([VIII.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yjersaq3gl77)).

3. **Progression Outcomes.** Most of the time, resolving story progression involves flipping the Story card to the other side (from A to B) or referring to a paragraph in your current Storybook or on another game element.

4. **Cards in a Cycle.** Every Cycle has 4 Story cards. Completing Story card 4 (A or B) is the only way to win a Cycle.

name: Doom.
rule:
For each Doom you gain, you must place 1 Doom token on your current Doom card.

1. **Unavoidable Doom.** Unavoidable Doom is Doom that cannot be mitigated or lowered in any way.

name: Doom Cards.
rule:
Doom cards show the slowly unfolding plans of the villain (or villains) of the Cycle and how they come to fruition. After completing a Cycle’s story introduction, you will gain Doom card 1 on side A (Doom Card 1A).

1. **Requirements.** Doom progression is resolved only if you fulfill all the requirements noted on your current Doom card. This is always a certain number of Doom tokens.

2. **Timing.** When you meet the current Doom card’s requirements, you will resolve its doom progression during the Doom Step ([VIII.8](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.d0k2kduejhv)).

3. **Progression Outcomes.** Resolving doom progression involves either flipping the Doom card to the other side or referring to a paragraph. Then, read the new Doom card (or the new side of the Doom card) and apply all listed effects.

4. **Cards in a Cycle.** Every Cycle has 3 Doom cards.

name: Winning.
rule: You only win if a Story Paragraph explicitly says so.

name: Losing.
rule:
There are many ways to lose. When you reach one of the conditions listed below, the game will direct you to a story paragraph that informs you how to proceed. If you are not directed to a specific paragraph, instead see Campaign Loss in your current Storybook.

* Resolving the final Doom card (3B) of the Cycle.

* Crew or Hull reaches 0

* Gaining Argo Fate over 9\.

* Getting to the end of the Timeline.

* Initiating a Battle with 0 Titans aboard the Argo.

* Other narrative-related events.

name: Active Argonauts.
rule: There are always exactly 4 active Argonauts, no matter how many players take part in a campaign. If you play with a smaller group, distribute the Argonauts between players as desired.

name: Argonaut Decision Making.
rule: The player controlling a given Argonaut always has the final say in all decisions regarding that Argonaut.

name: Argonaut Game Elements.
rule: Each Argonaut has their own Argonaut Sheet, Argonaut Portrait card, and Triskelion dial.

name: Least and Most Likely Argonaut.
rule: The “least likely Argonaut” is the Argonaut with the least amount of Mnemos cards. In case of a tie, the one with the least amount of Mnemos nodes. If there’s still a tie, choose randomly from the tied players. Conversely, the “most likely Argonaut” (or the ‘strongest’ Argonaut) is the Argonaut with the most Mnemos cards and Mnemos nodes. If there’s a tie, choose randomly from the tied players.

name: Skills.
rule: Each Argonaut has six Skills: Courage, Cunning, Endurance, Fury, Will and Wisdom. They have no inherent meaning and are just used for Tests.

name: Skill Value.
rule: Whenever you need to know the value of a particular Skill for an Argonaut, look at their portrait, Mnemos cards, Fated Mnemos cards, and notes section of their Argonaut sheet and add up all of the values for that skill shown in those places (all positive values except for Fated Mnemoses). For convenience, you may write the just calculated value in the Skills section of the Argonaut sheet to skip this process in the future, but make sure to update the Argonaut sheet if any of that Argonaut’s Mnemos cards, Fated Mnemos cards, or notes change.

name: Skill Modifiers.
rule: Any Rolls made with a Skill have the value of that Skill added to it. *(Skills can be negative.)*

name: Shared.
rule: Stats are shared between the Argonaut and the Titan they choose to take into Battle.

name: Persist After Adventures.
rule: Any Danger, Fate, or Rage gained in an adventure stays until instructed to lose it.

name: Triskelion Stats.
rule:
1. **Danger.** Danger is gained when you are attacked and also through suffering other negative effects. Many abilities and Gear cards become more powerful when you reach a certain Danger value.

1. **Danger Over 9\.** If you gain Danger over 9 during a Battle, perform a Trauma Draw ([VI.9.6](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.y85ji9ck2clm)), even if it wasn’t dealt during a Primordial Attack.

   2. **Fate.** Fate is gained to re-roll your dice and to pay for many abilities. High Fate may enable additional negative effects from Primordial Attacks.

1. **Fate Over 9\.** If you gain Fate over 9 during a Battle, immediately draw a Moiros card ([X.7.12](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.s2mf4gfl2nho)).

   3. **Rage.** Rage is gained each time you attack, giving you access to additional abilities from your Kratos Table.

   1. **Rage Over 9\.** If you gain Rage over 9 during a Battle, your Titan dies at the end of its current action. If it is not in the middle of one of its own actions, it dies immediately.

name: Over 9 Out of Battle.
rule: If any of your Triskelion stats goes over 9 outside of Battle, your Argonaut dies ([III.4.2](#bookmark=id.f0tf3em5m3bv)).

name: Leaving the Argo.
rule: A story effect may cause an Argonaut to leave the Argo (retire). When this happens, take a new Argonaut Sheet, draw a new Portrait card (or choose a Skill to apply a \+1 bonus to) and draw a random Mnemos card from the Mnemos Vault.

name: Argonaut Death.
rule:
1. **Causes.** Your Argonaut dies only when explicitly stated.

   2. **Rude Awakening.** When an Argonaut dies, immediately consult the Rude Awakening Special Event of your current Cycle, it will instruct you on how to create a new Argonaut.

3. **Save the Sheets.** Never discard or destroy the Argonaut Sheets of deceased Argonauts, as some events might reference them later.

name: Gaining Mnemoses.
rule: When you gain a Mnemos card, find the associated Mnemos card in the game box and write its title on your Argonaut Sheet. Then, write the paragraph number of the first Mnemos Breakthrough above the Breakthrough symbol on the corresponding Mnemos track.

name: Mnemos Cards.
rule:
1. **Back.** On the back of a Mnemos card you will find a title and an illustration, as well as a cryptic note as to its nature.

   2. **Front.** On its front, the card shows the Cycle number, three Mnemos levels, each with a corresponding ability and a Breakthrough paragraph number, as well as Skill bonuses for your Argonaut and the Mnemos traits.

   3. **Abilities.** You gain access to the Level 1 ability as soon as you gain the Mnemos card, while the Level 2 and Level 3 abilities are unlocked only after resolving the first and second Mnemos Breakthroughs, respectively.

   4. **Skills.** The Skill bonuses listed at the bottom of the card are applied onto your Argonaut Sheet upon gaining the Mnemos card. They are permanent, as long as you have the Mnemos card.

5. **Traits.** The 3 words at the top of a Mnemos card are its traits.

name: Mnemos Nodes.
rule: Mnemos nodes are like experience points for your individual Mnemos cards. Each Mnemos progress track goes from 0 to 10 nodes. A Mnemos Breakthrough is triggered whenever you reach 3, 7, or 10 Mnemos Nodes on a single Mnemos card. See also Gaining Mnemos Nodes ([IX.2.2.4](#bookmark=id.qfwgydoolayc)).

name: Mnemos Breakthroughs.
rule: When a Mnemos card’s Breakthrough is triggered, during the Story Step, resolve the Mnemos adventure code ([IX.2.5](#bookmark=id.koviu8d0r3rg)) (written above the Breakthrough symbol on the Mnemos progress track) and then unlock the next Mnemos Ability.

name: Mnemos Card Limit.
rule: Each Argonaut can have a maximum of 2 Mnemos cards. If an argonaut is instructed to gain a Mnemos card and is already at their limit, the Mnemos card goes to another random Argonaut instead. If all Argonauts already have 2 Mnemos cards, the new Mnemos is instead added to the Mnemos Vault.

name: Mnemos Vault.
rule:
The Mnemos Vault is a deck that consists of all Mnemos cards you have unlocked during your campaign, which are not currently assigned to any Argonaut. Keep it separate from other Mnemos cards.

1. **Return to Vault.** When an Argonaut dies or leaves the Argo for any reason, return their unresolved Mnemos cards to the Vault.

name: Resolved Mnemoses.
rule:
Once you resolve the last Breakthrough of a Mnemos card, it becomes resolved. This means that your Argonaut leaves the Argo or stays with a consequence. If they retire, this Mnemos does not return to the Mnemos Vault. Instead, Archive it.

1. **Katharsis.** Any Mnemos resolution which tells you to mark a Choice Matrix node is called a Katharsis. When you choose a Katharsis, write the Matrix code in the Katharsis box on your Argonaut Sheet.

name: Not Mnemoses.
rule: Fated Mnemos cards, though they have a similar namesake, are not considered Mnemos cards for the purposes of any effect.

name: Fated Mnemos Card Limit.
rule: Each Argonaut can have 2 Fated Mnemos cards at the same time. These do not count towards the limit of 2 Mnemos cards per Argonaut. If an Argonaut is instructed to gain a Fated Mnemos while already at their limit, they ignore that gain.

name: Gaining Fated Mnemoses.
rule: When you gain a Fated Mnemos, search the game box for all of the Fated Mnemos cards associated with the Cycle you are currently playing, shuffle them and draw a random card. Then, note it on your Argonaut Sheet similar to a regular Mnemos ([III.5.1](#bookmark=id.krj5mex9dqc0)). Fated Mnemos cards are always gained black side up.

name: Fated Mnemos Cards.
rule:
Each Fated Mnemos card includes a hindrance, Skill penalties for your Argonaut, and Mnemos traits.

   1. **Hindrance.** The hindrance is a negative effect the Argonaut must always adhere to. If the effects of your 2 Fated Mnemos cards contradict each other, the one you gained first takes precedence.

   2. **Skill Penalties.** The Skill penalties are simply applied onto your Argonaut Sheet. They are applicable as long as you have the Fated Mnemos card.

3. **Traits.** The 3 words at the top of a Fated Mnemos card are its traits.

name: Resolving Fated Mnemoses.
rule: If all the nodes to the left of the first breakthrough symbol are marked, resolve it during the Story Step. This simply involves flipping the card over, writing the blue side (trauma growth) ability on your Argonaut Sheet, then returning the card to the box. *(Fated Mnemoses do not have associated adventures.)*

name: Trauma Abilities.
rule: There is no limit to the amount of trauma growth abilities a single Argonaut can have. *(You can even gain the same trauma growth ability more than once and their effects will stack.)*

name: Afflictions.
rule: Afflictions introduce additional effects that are similar to Conditions ([X.7.11](#bookmark=id.ey853xr1zxv8)) but are tied to the Argonauts.

name: Boons.
rule: Boons, though rarer, are the positive equivalent of Afflictions. These can come in the form of personal traits, equipment or allies. Note these on your Argonaut Sheets.

name: No Components.
rule: Afflictions and Boons do not have physical components ([I.1.7.6](#bookmark=id.oggt789sv77y)).

name: Permanent.
rule: Afflictions and Boons are permanent— you can only remove them if an effect explicitly tells you to.

name: Types of Afflictions.
rule:
1. **Mazetouched.** No inherent effects.

2. **Consumed by Rage.** A Titan with the Consumed by Rage affliction must apply all Fate-Gated ([I.2.15](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.4j23mb42wgyz)) Primordial effects, regardless of their Fate value.

3. **Black Breath.** A Titan with the Black Breath affliction has \-1 Ambrosia Limit ([X.7.14.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.9o49rzek5dnh)).

4. **Fractured.** A Titan with the Fractured affliction must apply all Fate-Gated ([I.2.15](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.4j23mb42wgyz)) Primordial effects, regardless of their Fate value. In addition, they cannot gain nodes for Fated Mnemoses.

5. **Moirai Mistake.** A Titan with the Moirai Mistake affliction has “Crit Miss: Immediately full miss the Attack.”

6. **Abyss Curse.** A Titan with the Abyss Curse affliction must gain 1 Ambrosia token at the start of each of their turns in Battle.

7. **Goldthirst Curse.** A Titan with the Goldthirst Curse affliction must gain 1 Midas token at the start of each of their turns in Battle.

8. **Pursuer Curse.** A Titan with the Pursuer Curse affliction must turn 1 Midas token into 1 Ambrosia token at the start of each of their turns in Battle. If they cannot do this, they die.

9. **Windblight Curse.** A Titan with the Windblight Curse affliction gains \+1 Speed, \+1 to all involuntary movements suffered, and whenever they would gain Knockdown, they gain Float instead.

10. **Truth of the Primal Dark.** If you start your turn outside of all Titans’ range of lights, gain 1 Despair.

11. **Truth of Imminent Drowning.** At the start of each Battle, flip 1 Oxygen token.

12. **Truth of the Inevitable Failure.** You have the follow effects:

* Full Miss: Gain 1 Despair.
* Crit Miss: Gain 1 Despair.
* BPs you attack gain “Fail: gain 1 Fate”.

13. **Truth of Eternal Loneliness.** Awakening Lock. You do not benefit from IO40 or adjacency effects. You cannot use Hope tokens.

14. **Truth of the Unbearable Responsibility.** When you go first during the Titan Round, gain the Priority Target, or become the Party Leader, gain 1 Despair.

name: Gaia-born.
rule: All Dreamwalker-class Titans may also be referred to as Gaia-born Titans.

name: Argo-bred.
rule:
All Titan classes besides Dreamwalker are collectively referred to as Argo-bred Titans.

1. **Titan Restrictions.** There are a few restrictions relating to Argo-bred Titans:

1. **Limited Breeding.** You cannot breed ([VIII.6.7.1a](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.y85a1v8vssz4)) a Titan of a given Argo-bred class if you already have one, but you can still gain it from other sources, such as adventures or fusing ([VIII.6.7.2a](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.y85a1v8vssz4)).

   2. **One Type Per Battle.** You can only take one Titan of a given Argo-bred class into Battle, regardless of the number of Titans of that class you have.

name: Titan Power.
rule: Power die you include in every Power Roll you perform. Some Titans have Keywords next to their Power die that apply to all of their Attacks.

name: Speed.
rule: A value used for determining the distance a Titan’s Movement Action can go ([X.4.3](#bookmark=id.bu88suelpwdu)).

name: Ability Boxes.
rule: Abilities available to you while you are junctioned with this Titan.

name: Gear Slots.
rule:
A Titan has a certain number of Gear slots that determine how many of each Gear type it may equip. By default, a Titan has 2 Hand slots, 1 Armor slot, 2 Support slots, and 3 Attachment slots (onto the Titan sheet).

1. **Gear and Slots in Battle.** If a Titan’s Gear or Gear slots are changed during a Battle, currently equipped Gear cards must be discarded to accommodate the new slots types or limits, if necessary.

name: Abilities.
rule: Some Trauma Tables (particularly on Pattern cards) have ability boxes at the bottom of the table. This ability box is separate from the Titan Sheet.

name: Stacking.
rule: You must apply the benefits of your current Rage level, as well as all preceding levels, on a Titan’s Kratos table during each of its Titan Attacks.

name: Kratos Table Abilities.
rule:
Similar to constant abilities, Kratos Table abilities do not have a cost, but they are resolved differently. Most Kratos Table abilities modify the Titan’s Attack, by giving you access to either additional keywords or unique effects.

1. **Keyword Icons.** Kratos Table icons representing keywords should be treated as such. *(All Kratos Table symbols are explained on the back of the corresponding Titan Sheet or Pattern card.)*

2. **Options.** Most Kratos Table levels give you a choice of two options. These are always exclusive: If you decide to apply one of these options, you cannot apply the other. Options may include more than one effect. Kratos Table symbols that are linked with a “+” symbol are both part of the same option.

3. **Kratos Table Golden Rule.** You do not have to use any of your Kratos Table abilities until you reach the second ability window, at which point you must activate all applicable effects from your Kratos Table (1 option per level) that are still available. *(If an ability text says that “you may” do something, it is never forced. You can apply this ability, then decide not to resolve its effect.)*

name: Effects That Ask for Named Titans.
rule: Some effects may require you to note a Titan’s name somewhere (like the Timeline). In such a case, if the Titan is not named, you must immediately name it as part of resolving this effect.

name: Pattern Cards.
rule:
Patterns will override a Titan’s Trauma Table or Kratos Table and sometimes introduce new rules or abilities.

   1. **Always Named.** Whenever a Titan is assigned a Pattern, they must get a name.

2. **Multiple Patterns.** A Titan can have more than one Pattern, but if both Patterns relate to the same game element, the newer one will override the older (and that card will be discarded).

   3. **In Cycle I.** At the start of Cycle I, Patterns are unique and limited: a Titan assigned a Pattern from a Story effect gains it permanently, and when it dies, that Pattern is archived.

4. **In Cycle II.** Once you unlock the Gymnasion deck in Cycle II, gain Pattern cards you archived up until that point and put them in that deck.

   5. **Cycle-Specific Dreamwalker Patterns.** Every Cycle after Cycle 1 has its own default Dreamwalker pattern. These patterns are automatically applied to every Dreamwalker-class Titan gained in those Cycles. There is no limit to the number of Titans that can have these default patterns applied, and you cannot permanently lose them. They cannot be in the Gymnasion deck and they cannot be manually assigned.

   1. **Cycle-Specific Promo Dreamwalkers.** If you own any Cycle-specific Dreamwalker Titan Sheets, you may use those instead of the Cycle-specific Dreamwalker patterns. If you do, use only the Dreamwalker Titan Sheets for the Cycle you are currently in. The default Dreamwalker Titan Sheets are considered specific to Cycle 1 in this case.

name: Weapons (One-, Two-, and Three-Handed).
rule:
Weapons are any Gear cards that occupy Hand slots. Weapons can occupy one, two, three, or four Hand slots.
1. **One-Handed with Multiple Slots.** Some weapons have multiple single-hands listed in their slot type. These weapons take up the shown number of hand slots, but are considered One-Handed weapons for all other purposes.

name: Armor.
rule: Armor cards are Gear cards that occupy a Titan’s Armor slot. Each Armor Gear occupies one Armor slot.

name: Support.
rule: Support cards are Gear cards that occupy Support slots. Each Support Gear occupies one Support slot.

name: Attachments.
rule:
Attachment cards are Gear cards that can only be equipped by attaching them to either a Titan Sheet or to a non-Attachment Gear card. A Titan Sheet can have up to three Attachments and each non-Attachment Gear card can have one Attachment.
1. **Game Elements with Attachments.** If a game element is Exhausted, Discarded, Lost, or otherwise unequipped from a Titan, that element’s attachments suffer the same effect.

name: Offensive (Gear) Statistics.
rule:
Offensive Statistics are gear attributes found primarily on weapons and influence your ability to Attack. They are always on the left side of the Gear card and include:
   1. **Attack dice.** Number of Attack dice rolled during a Titan Attack. *(The symbol has a Power showing on the die face.)*
   2. **Precision bonus.** A value you add to the result of each of your Attack dice.
   3. **Weapon Power.** Number and tier (color) of Power dice provided by each hit. Once you hit the Primordial, for each successful hit you add this many dice to your Power Pool.
4. **Bonus Power.** Bonus Power di(c)e and/or raw Power that gets added to each Titan Attack as long as the Titan gets at least one hit.

name: Defensive (Gear) Statistics.
rule:
Defensive Statistics are gear attributes found primarily on armor, shield weapons, and some support cards. They are always on the right side of the Gear card and include:
   1. **Armor dice:** Number of Armor dice added to your Armor Roll dice pool. *(The symbol has a Potential showing on the die face.)*
   2. **Evasion bonus:** A value you add to the result of each of your Evasion dice.
   3. **Evasion re-roll:** During an Evasion Roll, you may re-roll a number of dice up to this value without any additional cost.
4. **Laser Resistance X.** (Cycle 4+ only, see [A.50](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.ulwxb3zhu135) for the Keyword) Each Laser Attack and Eclipse Attack hit deals X less Danger to you.
   5. **Sun Resistance X.** Each Sun Attack and Eclipse Attack hit deals X less Danger to you.
6. **Midas Resistance X.** This gear’s Midas token limit is increased by X (see [X.7.16.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.7dxryujxokoz)).
   7. **Microwave Resistance X.** Each Microwave Attack hit deals X Danger less to you.
   8. **Despair Resilience X.** When applying Despair effects, ignore X Despair tokens.
9. **Pain Resilience X.** Ignore the effects of X Pain tokens.

name: Gear-Specific Gate Requirements.
rule:
Most Gear cards have Gates ([I.2.15](#bookmark=id.4j23mb42wgyz)).
   1. **Fate/Rage/Danger.** If a Gate lists a Triskelion stat, compare it to the Titan that has the card with the Gate effect equipped. That Gate requirement is met if that Titan has at least that much of the specified stat.
   2. **Labyrinth Set.** If a Gate shows a Labyrinth symbol, compare it to the Titan that has the card with the Gate effect equipped. That Gate requirement is met if that Titan has at least the specified amount of Gear with the *Labyrinth* trait equipped.
   3. **Token.** If a Gate shows a token symbol of any kind, compare it to the Titan that has the card with the Gate effect equipped. That Gate requirement is met if that Titan has at least the specified amount of the shown token type.
   4. **Condition.** If a Gate shows a Condition symbol, compare it to the Titan that has the card with the Gate effect equipped. That Gate requirement is met if that Titan has the specified Condition card. If no Condition card is specified, the requirement is met if the Titan has any Condition card. If it specifies “Negative Condition”, the requirement is not met by the *Bellerophonic*, *Roused*, *Enriched*, *Enlightened*, *Lander*, *Bravery*, or *Resolve* Condition cards.
5. **Moiros.** If a Gate shows a Moiros symbol, compare it to the Titan that has the card with the Gate effect equipped. That Gate requirement is met if that Titan has drawn a Moiros card in this Battle.

name: Battle Only.
rule: All abilities on Gear cards are ignored until step [X.2.2.6](#bookmark=id.e2uul988obo1) of gear setup, and immediately are ignored again after resolving Battle Aftermath. Effects on the reverse side of non-Transforming Gear are not affected by this rule.

name: Weapon Traits.
rule:
Weapon traits include: *Axe*, *Blade*, *Bow*, *Cestus*, *Club*, *Discus*, *Hammer*, *Javelin*, *Khopesh*, *Knife*, *Shield*, *Slabsword*, *Spear*, *Sword*, *Whip, Kusarigama, Lance, Scythe, Pistol, Lightningthrower, and Harpoon*.
1. **Multiple.** If a weapon has multiple weapon traits, when you declare it as an active weapon ([X.5.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.1rcns5d3xa6j)), choose one of its weapon traits and treat it as if it *only* had that weapon trait for the duration of that Titan Attack Sequence.
2. **None.** Treat weapon cards with no weapon traits as having the *Other* weapon trait.

name: Material Traits.
rule: Material traits are all non-weapon traits.

name: Versatile.
rule: Fists can be used One- or Two-Handed.

name: One Per Titan.
rule: Each Titan can use a single Fists card. If a Titan has a Fists card and loses it, they cannot equip another one that Battle.

name: Equipping Fists.
rule: If, after performing Gear Loadout ([X.2.2.6](#bookmark=id.e2uul988obo1)), a Titan has one or more free Hand slots, you may equip a Fists card and use it as a One- or Two-Handed weapon. When a Titan is about to declare an active weapon during a Titan Attack, you may choose to discard one or more of that Titan’s equipped weapon cards ([I.1.4.1](#bookmark=id.i8z8qo1d9kbz)) to equip a Fists card.

name: Gaining Weapon During Battle.
rule: If, at any point of the Battle, a Titan gains a weapon card that uses up their hand slot(s) occupied by Fists, you must set the Fists card aside. It is then no longer equipped on the Titan.

name: Suntanned Fists.
rule: If you are in Cycle 4 or later, use the *Suntanned Fists* card instead of the standard *Fists*. They follow all the same rules.

name: Exhausted Gear.
rule: Exhausted Gear cannot be used as an active weapon ([X.5.1](#bookmark=id.1rcns5d3xa6j)), used for its defensive statistics ([V.2.2](#bookmark=id.2nvs9t1y8i71)), nor can its active abilities be used.

name: Exhaustible.
rule: Some game effects refer to Exhaustible Gear. Exhaustible Gear includes all Gear cards with the Cumbersome keyword or with active abilities that include Exhausting in their cost.

name: Backside.
rule: The back of Gear cards includes information about which Cycle the Gear card belongs to, the Technology card that allows you to craft the Gear, as well as some flavor text. Some Gear cards may also include additional rules there and other Gear cards are double-sided, with different names on either side.

name: Power Level.
rule: Each Gear card has a Power Level, which is a number that defaults to the number of the current Cycle.

name: Penalties.
rule:
When you use a Gear card with a Power Level that is lower than the number of the current Cycle, that Gear card suffers all of the following penalties:
   1. **No Tokens.** If the Gear card is a weapon, you cannot use nor place any Kratos tokens while using it as an Active Weapon.
   2. **No Power Reroll.** The Gear card cannot benefit from Power Reroll X.
   3. **Increased Cost.** Increase the Fate cost of all abilities on the Gear card by X, where X is the number of the current Cycle minus 1\. *(If there is no Fate cost initially, this does not add one.)*
   4. **Reduced Armor.** If the Gear card is Armor or a Shield, during Armor rolls, ignore 1 rolled Potential.
   5. **Reduced Evasion Rerolls.**  The Gear card has \-1 to its listed Evasion Rerolls.
6. **Reduced Evasion Bonus.**  The Gear card has \-1 to its listed Evasion Bonus.

name: Types of Primordials.
rule:
1. **Regular Primordials.** There are usually two regular Primordials in each Cycle, as noted on the Evolution Track on the Argo Sheet. You can expect to face these at regular intervals ([VIII.5.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.l0wy2sv6tayn)). As the campaign progresses, they will evolve and become more powerful ([VI.2.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.ghcoxcjb975q)).

2. **Adversary.** Adversaries are special Primordials that hunt you on the Voyage Map (see [VI.13](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.2nsv6h5kqy9e) for more).

3. **Boss Primordial.** Boss Battles may be triggered from various Story and Doom adventures. Each Cycle leads to a final showdown with the boss Primordial.

name: Primordial Facing.
rule:
Every Primordial miniature has a front, which is the direction it is facing. Which side of the Primordial is the front is up to you, it is just important to stick with whatever choice you make.

1. **In Front.** To find out if a game element is in front of the Primordial, draw an imaginary line passing along the edges of the spaces occupied by the Primordial miniature’s front. All spaces beyond this line in the direction the Primordial is facing are considered to be in front. Adjacency is not required to be considered in front.

2. **In Rear.** Rear designates only the spaces adjacent to the Primordial which are on the opposite side of the model from the front. These are marked with the Rear space symbol on the Primordial diagram.

name: Primordial Diagram.
rule: The Primordial diagram shows the number of spaces the Primordial occupies on the Battle Board (four or nine), as well as its front and Rear spaces and any other special spaces it may have ([VI.3.1](#bookmark=id.jbprxpxl8qec)).

name: Levels.
rule: Primordials have a Level which is most often tied to its Evolution or Battle Track during campaign play, and to the Tree of Knowledge for the Mnestis Theatre. A Primordial’s Level determines its Attribute values and any Traits it may have.

name: Level Boxes.
rule: Each Primordial sheet has a list of 9 Level Boxes. A Level Box holds all the information regarding a particular Primordial Level, which includes the Level numeral, Main Attributes Bonuses, a list of added or removed Traits, and, sometimes, a definition of a newly introduced Trait.

name: Level Attribute Bonuses.
rule:
The following is a list of Attribute Bonuses from Level Boxes. These do not stack with previous bonuses.

1. **AT bonus.** Increase the AT of each BP card by this value.

2. **Danger bonus.** Increase the Danger dealt by each Primordial Attack Sequence (or each hit, if stated as such) by this value. Only increase the Danger of hits that already deal Danger.

3. **Evasion dice bonus.** Increase the number of Evasion dice that need to be rolled for each Primordial Attack Sequence ([VI.9.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.r6ldr4fs8qmg)) by this value. *(Note that this does not apply to Judgements, because they do not use Evasion dice)*.

4. **Escalations.** At the start of Battle, Escalate the Primordial a number of times equal to this value.

5. **Danger/Fate Bonus.** Increase the Fate dealt by each Primordial Attack Sequence (or each hit, if stated as such) by this value. If the Attack Sequence does not deal Fate, increase the Danger instead. Only increase the Danger/Fate of hits that already deal Danger/Fate (respectively).

name: Evolution and Battle Tracks.
rule: Most Battles will tell you to either mark one Evolution box or one Battle box on the respective Primordial’s track. The numeral in the box you mark is the Level used for that Primordial fight. *(Adversaries and Bosses have no numbers because they are always considered Level 1.)*

name: Basic Traits.
rule:
Basic Traits are universal Primordial features. A Primordial may have any number of Basic Traits.

1. **Unavoidable Knockback.** Unavoidable Knockback is an effect that is triggered every time a Primordial ends its movement on a Titan ([VI.8.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.rbvtv9qklhnl)). The amount of Knockback is listed on the Primordial sheet.

1. **Resolving.** Resolve Unavoidable Knockback the same as Knockback X ([A.48](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.1fll06srtkv)), but it cannot be avoided or modified in any way. When a Titan suffers Unavoidable Knockback, you choose a direction that makes the Titan move immediately away from the Primordial, without moving through it.

2. **Blindspot.** Blindspots are spaces the Primordial cannot see, shown on the Primordial Diagram. Titans in Blindspots are excluded from targeting, unless the targeting specifically mentions Blindspots. Titans in the Blindspot gain \+1 Precision.

3. **Weakspots.** Weakspots don’t have any inherent effects of their own, but other effects may cause them to gain effects.

4. **Lumbering.** A Primordial with Lumbering cannot suffer Pushback or Knockback.

name: More on Traits.
rule: You will find some Traits on the Primordial Sheet itself, while others will be represented by Primordial Trait cards. Each Primordial Level specifies which Traits to use, only use those specified. Most of the Traits are additive, meaning that as the Level rises, they accumulate. Some Traits may be removed permanently when certain conditions are met.

name: Trait Cards.
rule: Many Primordials will use Trait cards to define their traits, usually when the Primordial Sheet doesn’t have enough space or the Trait is used across multiple Primordials. If a Trait is listed on a Primordial Level Box but is not defined on the sheet, find the matching Trait card and add it to the Battle. Trait cards can also be added to any Battle if instructed.

name: To-Hit.
rule: The value a Titan needs to hit the Primordial when Attacking it ([X.5](#bookmark=id.e8h6h6ly3clq)).

name: Speed.
rule: Maximum number of spaces the Primordial can travel with a single move.

name: Wounds.
rule: The number of Wounds required to slay the Primordial.

name: Not AI Cards.
rule: Both the Signature and the Routine may behave like an AI card, but they are not AI cards.

name: Triggering Signature.
rule: If another card, Trait, or any other effect instructs you to perform Signature, resolve the Primordial Attack on the Signature card.

name: Triggering Routine.
rule: The Routine is an action the Primordial performs when there is no eligible Target for any of the targeting lines on its current Attack card.

name: Routine Versions.
rule: Most Routines have a Mnestis Theatre version on the back. Always use the regular Routine during your campaign battles, and the Mnestis Theater version during Mnestis Battles.

name: Card Level.
rule:
AI Cards have a Level, denoted by a Roman numeral “0”, “I”, “II”, or “III” to mark it as a Level 0, 1, 2, or 3 AI card, respectively.

1. **AI 0\.** AI 0 cards cannot be discarded or removed until resolved (see also [X.5.10.8](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.57nsruu2dakl) and [X.2.2.8](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.xgoqsq3kblmy)).

name: Window of Opportunity.
rule: AI Cards, as well as BP, Signature, Routine, and any other Primordial game elements, may have the Window of Opportunity symbol on them in front of any effect. These are a time frame in which any number of Titan Reactions can be used. During a single window of opportunity, each Titan may use any number of Reaction abilities in any order. All Reactions must happen before the effect on the game element with the symbol.

name: Resolving an AI card.
rule:
To resolve an AI card, first check if it is an Attack Card. If it is, resolve it as such. Otherwise, resolve it as regular effects.

1. **Draw an AI Instructions.** If any effect instructs you to draw an AI card, first finish resolving the current action, then draw and resolve an AI card.

name: Resolving an Attack.
rule:
To resolve a Primordial Attack, perform the following steps in order.

1. **Step 1: Targeting.** Resolve the targeting lines ([VI.7.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.po74bek8dnb0)).

2. **Step 2: Primordial Attack.** Resolve a Primordial Attack Action ([VI.9](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.w2ger9p9mdsi)).

3. **Step 3: After Attack.** Resolve After Attack effects, if any ([VI.7.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.ko5jfh83ha1l)).

name: Targeting.
rule:
Targeting is a series of instructions called “lines”. Each line consists of the word “Target:” followed by targeting restrictions which are used to select the Target(s) for the Attack. *(A single targeting line can in fact take up more space than a row of text.)*

1. **Resolving.** To resolve targeting, first consider all Titans and spaces as possible Targets, then apply each targeting restriction on a line. Any possible Targets that do not meet at least one restriction of a line are removed from consideration for that line. If this removes all possible Targets from consideration, restart this process but on the next line. If this leaves any possible Targets, one of them becomes the Target. If you reach the last line and there are still no viable Targets, end this Attack resolution and resolve Routine instead (see [VI.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.m32mqojs6yn)).

2. **Priority Target.** The Titan with the Priority Target token ([X.1.12](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.3m9hmnbj8xol)) is considered to be the Priority Target and is a tiebreaker for Targeting. If two or more Titans are ever equally viable Targets (fulfill all instructions of the same targeting line) but one of them is the Priority Target, the Priority Target becomes the Target.

3. **Becoming a Target.** Each game element selected by the successful targeting line becomes a Target. A Titan that becomes a Target in the Targeting step remains the Target until the end of this Attack.

   4. **Outside Effects.** When a game effect other than a Primordial Attack instructs you to resolve a Primordial Attack against a specific Target, that effect overrules any targeting instructions on the indicated card.

   1. **Zone Target.** When an Attack with a Zone Target is changed to a single Target, the Zone is now the single space under the Target regardless of their position *(which means something like Reflex will not get you out of it because the Zone will move with you)*. The Attack retains all other properties *(e.g. you can still be Shaded from a Sun Attack)*.

5. **Restrictions.** Any number of the following can be strung together in any order on a single line. All such restrictions apply simultaneously.

   1. **Adjacent.** The Target must be on a space that shares an edge with a space occupied by the Primordial.

   2. **Board.** The Target is all spaces on the Board.

   3. **Closest.** The Target must be the one that is the shortest distance from the Primordial among all possible Targets.

   4. **Furthest.** The Target must be the one that is the longest distance from the Primordial among all possible Targets.

5. **In front.** The Target must be on any space in front ([VI.0.2.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.5l1kqw7sj2a6)) and in Line of Sight ([X.1.11](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.uhlbsa6j3qmu)) of the Primordial.

   6. **In range.** The Target must be within a distance that would allow the Primordial to perform the Attack against it after Moving. *(If the Attack is a Zone, the Target needs to be in the Zone after Primordial movement.)*

7. **In sight.** The Target must be within LoS of the Primordial ([X.1.11](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.uhlbsa6j3qmu)).

   8. **In/on X.** The Target must be in/on the specified area or space.

   9. **Last to Wound.** The Target must be the Titan that was last able to successfully Wound.

   10. **Least/Most X.** The Target must be the Titan with the least/most of the specified statistic, resource, or game element relative to other possible Targets. A Titan with none of something is not considered, but a Titan with zero of something is. *(For example, a Titan with no Despair tokens doesn’t have a Despair amount, whereas a Titan with zero Fate has a Fate amount, it’s just zero.)*

   11. **Not.** Inverses the following Targeting instruction.

   12. **No Target.** If a targeting line with this instruction is reached, resolve the effects that follow it and skip the rest of that Primordial Attack resolution. *(Does not trigger Routine because a Targeting line still resolved.)*

   13. **Priority Target.** The Target must be the Priority Target.

   14. **With X.** The Target must possess the specified statistic, resource, or game element.

   15. **X Titans.** Up to X Titans can become Targets.

   16. **Zone.** All spaces in the designated Zone are Targeted.

6. **Rotate.** “Rotate:” targeting instructions turn the Primordial to face the feature listed after the colon. After resolving a “Rotate:” line, continue with the “Target:” lines as normal. If there is no viable Rotate Target, perform Routine.

name: After Attack.
rule:
1. **Always Resolve.** Always resolve the After Attack effects unless the Target performed a Chain Break or died ([X.7.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.sorfk1pu6v44)).

2. **Same Target(s).** After Attack instructions are resolved against each Target.

3. **After Final Attack.** If a Primordial Attack includes an After Final Attack instruction, the After Final Attack instead only takes effect after resolving all Attack Sequences queued by that Attack.

name: Resolving Primordial Movement.
rule:
When instructed to Move the Primordial, do one of the following based on the effect:

1. **From Attack Action.** When a Primordial Attack Action or Judge Action instructs you to move the Primordial, do so up to a number of spaces equal to the Speed value

   1. **Shortest Path.** Always Move the Primordial along the shortest possible path to the Target of the AI card.

   2. **Direction.** If the Primordial is not in a straight line from its Target, Move it in such a way that it zig-zags until it is in a straight line from the Target, then continue the Move.

   3. **One at a Time.** Always move the Primordial one space at a time to resolve any triggered effects.

   4. **Unhindered.** Primordial movement is not hindered by any other game element.

   5. **Stopping.** The Primordial stops its Move when in range (for Ranged Attack Actions), when adjacent (for any other Attack Actions), or when it has moved a number of spaces equal to its Speed.

2. **Other.** When any effect besides an Attack Action or Judge Action instructs you to move the Primordial, do so in the indicated direction. If this would cause it to move past the Board Edge, continue to move it along the edge prioritizing a direction that will lead it away from the most Titans (if possible).

name: Change Facing.
rule: At the beginning and end of movement, the Primordial always turns to face the Target, unless stated otherwise.

name: Destructible Terrain.
rule: When a Primordial moves onto a space containing a Destructible Terrain tile, that tile is Destroyed and removed from the board.

name: Through Titan spaces.
rule: When a Primordial moves through a space containing a Titan, that Titan suffers CRASH.

name: Stopping on Titan spaces.
rule: When a Primordial ends movement on a space containing a Titan, that Titan suffers CRASH and Unavoidable Knockback ([VI.3.1.1](#bookmark=id.842mk8bofqxf)).

name: Moving Towards Missing Components.
rule: When you are about to move the Primordial relative to another object on the Board, if that object is not there, ignore that movement unless stated otherwise *(e.g. Retreat X)*. While moving the Primordial relative to another object (like Titan) on the Board, if that object ceases to be on the Board, immediately end that movement.

name: Variants.
rule:
1. **Jump.** Move as normal, but ignore VI.8.3.

2. **Appear Behind.** Appear in Front, but the Primordial moves to the furthest space adjacent to the Target.

3. **Appear in Front.** Jump, but the Primordial has Unlimited Speed.

4. **Land on Top.** Appear in Front, then Pushback 1\. Resolve the Pushback as if it was part of the movement[15](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.qii5k8xaqhgh).

5. **Move Lateral.** Check the four cardinal directions from the Primordial for the shortest straight line that would end with the Target(s) of the Attack Action in a straight line from the Primordial. Move the Primordial that way.

6. **Move to LOCATION.** The Primordial has Unlimited Speed for this movement, and moves to LOCATION instead of towards the Target(s).

7. **Move X.** Move as normal, but use X instead of the Primordial’s Speed value.

name: Resolving a Primordial Attack Action.
rule:
To Resolve a Primordial Attack Action, do the following in order:

1. **Step 1: Move.** If there is a Move instruction, resolve a Primordial Move ([VI.8.1.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.twjj61avo4un)).

2. **Step 2: Check Range.** Check if the Target(s) are in range of the Attack Action (adjacent by default). If they are not, stop resolving the Attack Action. *(Proceed to After Attack.)*

   1. **Out of Reach (OoR).** If a Target was in range before Step 1 but no longer is, the Target is considered Out of Reach (or OoR for short) and the Primordial Attack Action ends immediately.

3. **Step 3: Determine Victims.** All Targets and all Titans within the Attack Zone (if there is one) become Victims of this Attack.

4. **Step 4: Resolve Attack Banners.** Any Trigger banners connected to the Attack banner are resolved now. Connected banners have ‘\>’ between them, whereas banners listed after a banner that ends with ‘\]’ are resolved as Attack Consequences instead. *(An Attack banner that ends with ‘|’ therefore has no connected banners).*

5. **Step 5: Initiate Attack Sequences.** Queue ([X.7.23](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.dm55uudt7cpo)) a Primordial Attack Sequence against each Victim (as a Set ([X.7.23.1a](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.pixu2ak24fxy)) if there is more than one).

name: Attack Sequence.
rule:
To resolve a Primordial Attack Sequence, perform the following in order:

1. **Step 1: Turn.** Turn the Primordial towards the Target, unless the Target is a Zone.

   2. **Step 2: Evasion Roll.** In the upper right corner of the card that triggered this Attack Sequence, there is a number of Evasion dice and an Evasion Roll difficulty number. The Victim rolls the specified number of dice.

   1. **Rerolls.** If the Victim has any Evasion Rerolls (or wants to reroll with Fate), they may use them before counting hits.

3. **Step 3: Count Hits.** Add any Evasion bonus the Victim may have to each Evasion die rolled, then the Victim receives a number of hits equal to the number of die results that didn’t meet or beat the Evasion roll difficulty.

   1. **Full Evade.** If there are zero hits, it is a Full Evade. Trigger all “Full Evade:” abilities and skip Step 4 of this Attack Sequence.

4. **Step 4: Attack Consequences & Effects.** If a Titan is left with at least one hit, it must resolve all effects of the Attack Action.

name: Danger From Attacks.
rule:
When instructed to gain Danger from an Attack, you must first immediately perform an Armor Roll if you have any non-exhausted cards with Armor dice. Afterwards, gain Danger equal to value dealt minus the number of Potentials rolled in the Armor Roll. If a Titan gains at least 1 Danger this way, it immediately performs a Trauma Draw ([VI.9.6](#bookmark=id.y85ji9ck2clm)).

   1. **Armor Roll.** To perform an Armor Roll, roll a pool of Power dice equal to the total of all Armor dice on all of your equipped cards *(Remember, if you use a weapon to defend, you cannot use other equipped weapons during the same Attack)*.

2. **Massive Danger.** When you would gain Danger that would put you over 9, note the amount you should be, then raise Danger to 9 and treat the following Trauma Draw as if your Danger was increased to the noted value. If your Danger temporarily goes up to at least 14, 18, or 21 this way, add the respective Obol cards to the Obol deck immediately.
