Cards added to the Obol deck as a result of Massive Danger remain there until the end of Battle.

   1. **Danger 14+.** Add the “You died horribly” card to the Obol deck.

   2. **Danger 18+.** Also add the “Your gruesome death becomes legend” card to the Obol deck.

   3. **Danger 21+.** Also add the “Your death transcends reality” card to the Obol deck.

name: Other Attack Consequences.
rule:
Attack effects may deal Fate, Rage, tokens (e.g. Ambrosia or Despair), or Conditions. When this happens, simply gain the indicated resource or game element.

1. **Raising a Trauma Level.** When instructed to raise your Trauma Level by X, gain enough Danger to be in the next Trauma Threshold (as noted by your Trauma Table) and repeat X times. Treat the Massive Danger levels as higher Trauma Thresholds as necessary. If an Attack instructed you to do this, perform a Trauma Draw.

name: Trauma Cards.
rule:
There are four severity levels of Trauma cards: Minor, Major, Grave, and Obol, the same as the thresholds found in your Trauma Table.

   1. **Discarding.** When you discard a Trauma card, place it at the bottom of its corresponding deck.

   2. **Drawing (Not Trauma Draw).** When instructed to draw a specific Trauma card, resolve a Trauma Draw from the designated deck, ignoring your current Danger value.

3. **Positive/Negative.** All Trauma cards are either positive or negative, as denoted by the \+/- sign in the bottom right corner of the card. *You Live\!* Obols are positive traumas, and *You Died.* Obols are negative.

name: Trauma Draw.
rule:
To perform a Trauma Draw, consult your Trauma Table ([IV.4](#bookmark=id.de2lg2hr61lo)) and draw a Trauma card of a severity corresponding with your current Danger value. Resolve the Trauma card’s effects, then discard it.

   1. **Obols (and Obol Draw).** To Perform an Obol Draw, shuffle the Obol deck and draw the top card. Resolved and discarded Obols are always shuffled back into the Obol deck rather than being placed on the bottom. *(An Obol Draw is still a Trauma Draw.)*

2. **Simultaneous Obol Draws.** If you are ever forced to perform two or more Obol Draws at the same time, you die instead. *(Consecutive effects are not simultaneous.)*

   3. **Surviving a Trauma Draw.** A Titan “survives” a Trauma draw if they did not draw a Trauma card that instructed them to die.

4. **Trauma Cards that Draw Another**[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww)**.** When resolving a Trauma card that instructs you to draw another Trauma card, the entire sequence of resolutions counts as one trauma draw for the purposes of other effects.

name: Primordial Attack Types.
rule:
There are a number of variations on the basic Primordial Attack action, which change some aspect of how it resolves:

1. **Curse Attack.** (Cycle 4+) Curse Attacks do not have Targeting instructions. When resolving a Curse Attack, proceed directly to triggering its effects, which will describe who suffers the consequences of the Attack. The name of the action on a Curse Attack has no meaning.

2. **Eclipse Attack.** (Cycle 4+) Eclipse Attacks are Sun Attacks, except the Sun Edge is the same as the line drawn to determine the front of the Primordial. Something is opposite an Eclipse Attack’s Sun Edge (for the purposes of determining Shade) only if it is in front of the Primordial (i.e. there must be an obscuring Terrain tile or a Titan in between the space in question and the Primordial, and that Terrain tile or Titan must be adjacent to said space for that space to be considered Shaded).

3. **Judgement.** Judgement is an Attack type modifier that can be applied to any other kind of Attack or can be the sole type. When resolving a Judgement, skip step [VI.9.2.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.r6ldr4fs8qmg), treat the Evasion Roll as a Solo Test instead, and skip the Armor Roll [VI.9.3.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.fqzdx4rp9o9n) when resolving consequences.

   1. **Multiple Dice.** In the upper right corner of the card of a Judgement, if there is a number of dice and the Test difficulty, the Victim rolls the specified number of dice instead of just one.

2. **No Dice Listed.** If there is no dice count or difficulty listed on the top right of a Judgement Attack card, skip steps [VI.9.2.1-3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.r6ldr4fs8qmg) instead and treat all Victims as having received a Hit.

   3. **Hits Not Fails.** Each Fail during the Test is instead considered a Hit for the purposes of resolving consequences.

   4. **Test Bonus.** Add one of the two listed Argonaut Skill bonuses to all dice rolled as part of a Judgement Attack.

   5. **Evade Abilities.** All Full Evade, Crit Evade, and Crit Evade Fail abilities can still trigger off of Solo Tests caused by Judgements, but Evasion Rerolls cannot be used.

   6. **Not an Attack.** An Attack with the Judgement type is no longer considered an Attack for the purposes of other effects.

4. **Laser Attack.** (Cycle 3+) To perform a Laser Attack, draw a line between the Primordial and the Target. If this line crosses over a Titan or Obstacle Terrain tile that is adjacent to the Target, that Titan/Terrain tile immediately becomes the Target. If a Terrain tile becomes the Target this way, it is destroyed and the rest of the Attack Action is skipped.

   1. **Double/Triple Laser Attack.** Resolved as a Simultaneous Attack consisting of one Laser Attack originating from every front space. If any of these Lasers does not connect with a Target, the first crossed Obstacle Terrain becomes a Target.

   2. **Sideways Laser Attack.** When a Laser Attack instructs you to “continue sideways”, then, after hitting a *Black Ice* Terrain tile, resolve additional Laser Attacks (as Double/Triple Laser Attack) from the two sides of the tile orthogonal to the side hit. This Attack does not destroy *Black Ice* tiles.

   3. **Omnilaser Attack.** Resolved as a Double/Triple Laser Attack, but the Lasers originate from every side of the Primordial.

4. **Timefront Laser Attack.** Resolve a Laser Attack originating from every Timefront space (see Timefront, [C.3.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.oz7gl2wn3czy)).

   5. **Symbol.** In Cycle 4+, Laser Attack variations are denoted by a symbol instead of an Attack type. The number next to the symbol is the number of lasers drawn.

5. **Microwave Attack.** (Cycle 5\) Microwave Attacks follow all rules of Laser Attacks, but are not considered Laser Attacks. If a Microwave Attack is initiated from outside the Battle Board, it originates from the Board Edge closest to the Target, in the column the Target is in when the Attack Action resolves.

6. **Range Attack X.** Range Attacks can be performed against a Titan as long as it is within X spaces of the Primordial, instead of requiring adjacency.

7. **Regular Attack.** A Regular Attack is any attack type that is not one of the others listed here. They simply have “Attack” listed at the bottom of the card.

8. **Sequential Attack.** Sequential Attacks are performed against multiple Targets instead of one. Each Attack only gets queued if the previous one wasn’t ended by a Chain Break ([X.7.6](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yx8r43h369el)). Primordial Movement during this Attack does not cause CRASH.

9. **Simultaneous Attack.** Simultaneous Attacks are performed against multiple Targets instead of one. Queue Attacks as a set ([X.7.23](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.dm55uudt7cpo)).

10. **Sun Attack.** (Cycle 3+) Sun Attacks are Zone Attacks that have unique targeting, typically targeting the Unshaded Board.

   1. **Shaded.** Every space adjacent to a non-shaded Titan, Obstacle Terrain tile, or Obscuring Terrain tile that is opposite the Sun Edge is shaded.

   2. **Sun Edge.** The Sun Edge is the Board Edge opposite the Primordial’s Front.

11. **Wish Attack**. (Cycle 4+) Wish Attacks are Regular Attacks but the Evasion Roll and Armor Roll steps are ignored for the duration of the Attack.

12. **Zone Attack.** Zone Attacks are performed against a set of spaces instead of Titans; Queue (as a set) Attack Sequences against each Titan in the Zone.

   1. **Zone X.** Performed against all spaces within X spaces of the source of the Attack, including diagonally.

   2. **Diagram Zones.** Performed against the specific spaces shown on the diagram.

name: BP Card Components.
rule:
1. **Primordial Symbol:** Shows the Primordial the BP card belongs to.

   2. **Name:** Name of the Primordial body part you are attacking.

3. **Card Level.** BP Cards have a Level, denoted by a Roman numeral “I”, “II”, or “III” to mark it as a Level 1, 2, or 3 BP card, respectively.

4. **Primordial Resources:** On the top right of the card are the resources you will gain after the Battle if the BP card is in the Wound Stack.

5. **AT:** A value in the center of the card next to the AT symbol which is the Power required to Wound this BP.

6. **Responses:** All other effects on the BP card (sections preceded by “Fail”, “Wound”, “Instinct”, and “Critical”).

name: Creating VPs.
rule:
Until instructed to “create a VP” from an effect, the VP is ignored.

1. **Interchangeable Parts.** When a Primordial VP is created, remove the interchangeable part of the Primordial miniature and switch it with the Vantage Point part. Alternatively, if you don’t want to switch the parts, you can use the VP space marked on the Primordial Sheet instead. *(Not all VPs have interchangeable parts.)*

2. **No VPs.** If a Primordial has no associated VP description, you cannot create VPs on it by any means.

name: Climbing a VP.
rule: A Titan adjacent to a Primordial with a VP may perform a Climb test as a free action ([X.7.21](#bookmark=id.npzdepbhxnd7)). If you succeed, place your Titan on the VP space.

name: Falling down from a VP.
rule: At the end of the Primordial Round, most VPs force you to perform a Hold on test ([X.7.21](#bookmark=id.npzdepbhxnd7)) to find out if you fall down. The particular effects of falling from a VP are explained on the Primordial Sheet.

name: Movement and VPs.
rule: A Titan may move from a Primordial VP space to a space adjacent to the Primordial using a Movement Action, or any move-like ability, as if they were adjacent spaces. If a Titan suffers Knockdown or involuntary movement while on a VP space, they must first be Displaced to a space adjacent to the Primordial.

name: Occupancy.
rule: Only one Titan can occupy a single VP at a given time.

name: Adjacency and Range.
rule: Titans on VPs are considered adjacent and in range 1 to the Primordial and all Titans adjacent to this Primordial. They measure range to other Titans and game elements from the space occupied by the Primordial that is closest to that Titan or game element. Other Titans adjacent to the Primordial are not considered to be adjacent to Titans on VPs.

name: Introducing an Adversary.
rule: The Storybooks will instruct you on when to introduce an Adversary into your campaign. When an Adversary is introduced into a campaign, write its name on the Adversary Track.

name: Start of Cycle.
rule: The Adversary does not start a Cycle on the map. Ignore all Adversary activations until an event instructs you to introduce it.

name: Activating the Adversary.
rule:
Whenever you are instructed to resolve an Adversary symbol, resolve one of the following, checking each in order (if you resolve one of them, skip checking the rest).

1. **Check 1: On Map.** If the Adversary is already present on the Map, move it ([VI.13.6](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.k8bsuy6yr9hr)).

2. **Check 2: Exploration Step.** If the Adversary is not on the Map and one symbol is drawn during the Exploration Step (only), nothing happens.

   3. **Check 3: Otherwise.** If the Adversary is not on the Map, and you either triggered (drew) two symbols during a single Exploration Step or triggered one symbol during any other Step, spawn the Adversary: Place the small miniature of the current Adversary four tiles away from the Argo. If you can’t place it four tiles away, place it as far away as possible.

   1. **Only Spawn or Move.** If you spawned the Adversary during the Exploration Step, ignore all other Adversary Activation symbols until the end of the Exploration Step.

name: Resolve After Battles.
rule: If an Adversary activation happens during a Battle, resolve it immediately after the current Battle ends. Ignore this rule and the Adversary Activation if the Battle is against an Adversary.

name: Adversary Battles.
rule:
When told to resolve an Adversary battle, go to the corresponding Battle Scenario in the Storybook. Adversaries are not tied to a single Cycle and you may need to refer to other Cycles’ Storybooks.

   1. **Resolution Timing.** When an Adversary Battle is triggered, resolve it only at the end of your current Step.

2. **Argo Move Onto Adversary.** An Adversary Battle is only triggered from Adversary movement *(never Argo movement)*.

name: Adversary Movement Rules.
rule:
1. **Towards Argo.** The Adversary always moves one tile towards the Argo along the shortest available path.

2. **Trigger Battle.** If Adversary movement causes it to end on the same Map tile as the Argo, remove its miniature from the map and trigger an Adversary Battle. If the Adversary is already on the same tile as the Argo during an activation, it does not move but you still trigger the Adversary Battle.

3. **One Tile Per Symbol.** The Adversary moves one tile per Adverary symbol resolved.

4. **Only Revealed Tiles.** The Adversary can only move through revealed tiles.

5. **Ignore Tile Contents.** The Adversary can move onto any adjacent Map tile, even if it is not connected by a directional arrow. It ignores tile sectors and all Map symbols.

   6. **Direction Priorities.** If there is more than one equally viable path, the Adversary prioritizes horizontal movement as long as it brings it closer to the Argo. “Closer”, in this case, means along the shortest possible path of revealed map tiles (do not count distance of facedown/unplaced tiles).

   1. **In Cycle 2\.** In Cycle 2, instead, prioritize vertical movement.

name: Campaign Round.
rule: A Campaign Round is one in-game day on the Timeline.

name: Steps.
rule: Steps make up the structure of a Campaign Round and are always resolved in a fixed order.

name: Phases.
rule:
Phases are gameplay segments that differentiate which rules are in effect at any moment.

1. **Types.** There are three different Phases in ATO: Voyage, Battle, and Adventure.

2. **Rule of Thumb.** You are always in a game Phase. If you are not in the Adventure Phase or the Battle Phase, you are in the Voyage Phase.

3. **Entering Phases.** When you are in a Battle, you are in the Battle Phase. When you are reading passages in the Storybook, you are in the Adventure Phase. Always return to the Voyage Phase after finishing a Battle or Story section.

4. **Campaign Round Fluidity.** Any Campaign Round Step can consist of any combination of Phases.

name: Events.
rule: All Campaign Round Steps always happen, but there are not always events during a Step in a given Campaign Round. Steps VIII.1-3 always have events.

name: Always Move.
rule: During the Movement Step, you must move the Argo to an adjacent Map tile.

name: Map Arrows.
rule:
You must choose a direction from the arrows on your tile. You cannot move in a direction without arrows. If there is more than one arrow in the same direction, you must pick one before revealing the new tile, the tile in that direction will be Sectored.

1. **Factions on Arrows.** Faction symbols on Map Arrows tell you which Faction(s) are on the Map tiles your current tile connects to.

2. **Lock Symbols.** If a map arrow has a padlock symbol on it, ignore that arrow unless you have the required Technology.

   1. **River.** (Cycle 1 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the Shallows Navigation Technology card.

   2. **First Cataract.** (Cycle 2 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the XXX Technology card.

   3. **One-Way Canals.** (Cycle 2 only) These Map Arrow symbols represent directions in which you are free to move one way, but are Locked from the other. If you have the Up-Stream Navigation Technology, you may move through the Locked arrow.

   4. **Second Cataract.** (Cycle 2 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the XXX Technology card.

   5. **Sluice Gate.** (Cycle 2 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the Sluice Gate System Integration Technology card.

   6. **Time Wound.** (Cycle 3 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the Time Wounds Technology card.

   7. **Past Portal.** (Cycle 3 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the Trespass Protocol Technology card.

   8. **Future Portal.** (Cycle 3 only) Ignore Map Arrows with this symbol unless you have the Icebreaker Protocol Technology card.

name: Placing Map Tiles.
rule: If the tile you are moving to isn’t revealed, find the Map tile with the number of the arrow selected on its back and place it adjacent to the Map tile you are traveling from, so that the directional arrows on the shared border of the two tiles align. Make sure to orient the new tile in the same way as all of the other tiles. Place a reminder token of some sort on the tile to mark it as Unexplored.

name: Tile Sectors.
rule:
1. **Sectored Map Tiles.** Some map tiles are divided into sectors, marked with α and β symbols, representing bodies of water separated by land. The Argo can only be on one sector of the tile.

2. **Leaving.** You can only leave a sector by following the directional arrows marked with that sector's symbol.

3. **Moving Between Sectors.** Some Technology cards will allow you to move from one sector of a map tile to another. Doing so does not count as Argo movement, use up your Argo movement for the campaign day, nor does it advance the Timeline.

name: Local Faction.
rule:
The Local Faction is the Faction whose symbol is in the bottom right corner of the Map tile the Argo is currently on.

1. **Multiple Local Factions.** If there is more than one faction symbol on a tile and you are instructed to apply modifiers and triggers depending on the Local Faction, you must pick one of the Tile’s options for each such effect.

name: Start Day.
rule: During the Timeline Step, mark off the next empty box on the Timeline on your Argo Sheet.

name: No Boxes.
rule: If you need to mark an empty box and there aren’t any, your campaign is lost.

name: Timeline Events.
rule: If there are any symbols, events or other notes assigned to the current day, they must be resolved in the appropriate Campaign Round Step

name: Step 1: Resolving Exploration Symbols.
rule:
Resolve any appropriate Map Symbols

1. **Doom.** If there is a Doom symbol, gain 1 Doom.

2. **Progress.** If there is a Progress symbol and the tile is unexplored, gain 1 Progress.

3. **Titan.** If there is a Titan symbol and the tile is unexplored, gain 1 Titan (Dreamwalker).

4. **Daedalus Workshop.** XXX

5. **Spartan Stronghold.** (Cycle 2 only) If there is a Spartan Stronghold symbol, lose 1 Crew or 1 Hull.

6. **Frozen Time.** (Cycle 3 only) If there is a Frozen Time symbol and the tile is unexplored, gain 1 Frozen Time if you have the required Technology.

name: Step 2: Resolving an Exploration Draw.
rule:
To perform an Exploration Draw, draw a card from the top of the Exploration deck and resolve it. (*Do not shuffle drawn cards back into the deck until after ending the second stack.)*

1. **Resolving Exploration Cards.** To resolve an Exploration card, do the following in order:

1. **Step 1: Main Effect.** Resolve the Main Effect of the card. Unless stated otherwise, any Diplomacy triggers ([II.2.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.szoqxjhdnpcj)) always refer to the Local Faction ([VIII.1.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.f74cwg9o0jyi)).

2. **Step 2: Adversary Symbols.** Resolve Adversary symbols ([VI.13.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.fsiztu8icgpo)). If this triggers an Adversary Battle, resolve it after completing the Exploration Draw.

   3. **Step 3: Special Disposal Effect.** If there is a Special Disposal Effect, resolve it.

   4. **Step 4: Stack Symbol.** If there is an “End stack” symbol in the lower right corner of the card, end the stack. If this is the second time a stack has ended, reform the deck and end the Exploration Draw step. Otherwise (if there is a “Draw another” symbol), draw and resolve another Exploration card for the current stack.

2. **Reform Deck.** After resolving all Exploration cards and noting Adverary symbols, shuffle all drawn Exploration cards (that have not been removed from the deck) back into the deck.

name: Step 3: Performing Acclimations.
rule: If your current day has an Acclimation, search the Exploration deck for the cards with numbers corresponding to the list found on the left of the acclimation arrow and archive them. Then, find the Exploration cards with numbers listed on the right of the arrow, add them to the Exploration deck, and then shuffle it.

name: Adventures.
rule: If the Argo is on an Unexplored tile with an “Adventure” or “R\&R” symbol, resolve an appropriate Adventure ([IX.2](#bookmark=id.pp5lc7perm9y)).

name: Multiple Battles.
rule: If more than one Battle event happens on the same day, you may choose the order in which they are resolved.

name: Timeline Battles.
rule: For Timeline Battles, choose one of the two Regular Primordials (shown on the Evolution Track on the Argo Sheet) from your current Cycle, then proceed to the corresponding Battle Scenario in the Storybook ([X.2.1](#bookmark=id.n1j2ozqbwrrb)).

name: Other Battles.
rule: All Battle events that are not Timeline Battles will point you to a specific Battle Scenario.

name: Phase Change.
rule: Initiating a Battle changes the current Game Phase to the Battle Phase.

name: City.
rule: If the Argo is on a map tile with the City symbol but not the Last Visited City token, you may use any Negotiation Technology abilities ([VIII.6.4.2c](#bookmark=id.yq9r2cjz33s1)).

name: Technology Cards.
rule:
Technology Cards come in a variety of types and sizes, though the size of the card has no mechanical significance. Every Technology card is double-sided. One side shows the Technology’s Requirements as well as other Technologies that will become available for research once you have researched that card. The other side shows the benefits of researching the Technology, allowing you to craft new Gear or gain unique abilities.

1. **Technology Types.** Technologies are divided into three types: Core, Structural, and Battle.

2. **Technology Deck.** Technologies that you research are added to the Technology deck. Upon being added to the Technology deck, you gain access to all abilities and Production Facilities provided by that Technology.

   3. **Projects Deck.** Technologies that are available for research are added to the Projects deck. You gain no benefits from them but you can freely peek at them to see what you can gain by researching them.

4. **Gaining Projects.** When you add a Technology card to the Technology deck, immediately add all the Technology cards in its “Leads to” section to the Projects deck, including Technologies from other Cycles.

5. **Requirements.** In order to research a given Technology card, you must meet all of its requirements (listed in its “requirements” box). Each requirement is separated by a comma (“,”). Requirements should be treated as Triggers on effects that involve Research.

   1. **Technology Requirements.** Other required Technology cards must be in the Technology Deck.

   2. **Costs.** Some Technology cards have a cost which will be clearly described on the card. This cost must be paid in order to research that Technology.

   3. **‘OR’ Requirements.** If two of the requirements are separated by ‘OR’, it means that you only need to fulfill one of those two requirements in order to be able to research the card.

6. **Reserves.** Technologies that are neither in the Technology deck nor the Projects deck are considered to be in the Reserves and should be kept in the game box.

name: Technology Breakthroughs.
rule:
New Technology cards can only be researched on specific Timeline days called Technology Breakthroughs. These are marked by either the Structural Technology symbol or the Battle Technology symbol.

1. **Researching Tech.** If either Technology Breakthrough symbol is on your current day on the Timeline, research one Technology of the appropriate type from the Projects deck. To do so, simply move the chosen card to the Technology deck.

   2. **Gaining Technology Cards and Project Cards through Different Means.** Sometimes you will gain a Technology card from a source other than a Technology Breakthrough. In that case, you simply add it to your Technology deck (remember to add any Technology cards it Leads to to your Projects deck). Similarly, sometimes you will be instructed to add a Technology card to the Projects as a result of something other than another Technology’s “Leads to” box. In that case, you simply add it to your Projects deck.

3. **Not Cycle-Tied.** Although Technologies cards have an associated Cycle, you are not restricted by Cycle when researching Technology cards. *(If you have a card from a future cycle in your projects deck and you meet all of its requirements, you may research it.)*

name: Using Technologies.
rule:
1. **Core Technologies.** Core Technologies are any Technologies on tarot-sized cards. They should be treated as Structural Technologies for all purposes.

2. **Structural Technologies.** Structural Technology are cards that grant you abilities that come in a variety of types:

   1. **Permanent.** Permanent abilities introduce constant effects and rule changes. They are always active.

   2. **Immediate.** Immediate effects must be resolved the moment their respective Technologies are researched.

3. **Negotiation.** Negotiation abilities (those with the City symbol) may be used while at a City ([VIII.6.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.iq5vmecxpzyn)).

   4. **Save.** Save abilities may be used “on demand” whenever you need them. Each Save ability may only be used once per effect.

   5. **Reference.** Reference abilities include rule summaries and serve as a reminder. They do not introduce any additional effects of their own. These are primarily Core Technologies designed to ease you into the game or update the status quo for a new Cycle.

   6. **Other.** All other Technology abilities may only be used in the Advancement Step or when stated.

3. **Battle Technologies.** Battle Technologies are either Production Facilities or Argo Abilities ([X.4.11-13](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.7hyot354jrbw)).

   1. **Production Facilities.** Production Facilities give you access to new Gear cards and are only used for crafting.

4. **Technology Abilities.**

   1. **Active Ability Limit.** Each active Technology ability can be used once per day (once per Campaign Round), with the exception of Save abilities.

   2. **Cooldown.** Some Technology abilities include a “Cooldown: X days” instruction. When you use an Ability with that instruction, write its name down as an event on the Timeline, X days from now (your current day) with the word “Cooldown” in parentheses. Cooldown events have no effect when resolved, but the Ability that placed the event on the Timeline cannot be used until the event is resolved.

   3. **Tech Events are Always Advancements.** All Events added to the Timeline using Technologies are Advancement Events and should be resolved in the Advancement Step.

name: Trading.
rule:
Some game elements allow you to Trade resources.

   1. **Limited.** Each trading effect has a limited number of resource exchanges you can perform.

2. **Exchanges.** A single exchange is a trade of one resource type for another resource type, according to the ratio noted on the card. As a single exchange you can trade as many units of a resource type as you like.

name: Crafting Gear.
rule:
Crafting is the main source of new Gear cards that can greatly improve the combat capabilities – and survivability – of your Titans.

   1. **Any Number of Times.** You can craft multiple cards during every Advancement Step, provided you have the required resources.

2. **Must Be Producible.** You can only craft gear listed on the Production Facilities in your Technology deck

3. **Piece Limited.** You cannot have more copies of a gear card in the Armory than the game comes with.

   4. **Costs.** The cost of each card is printed on the Production Facility Technology card.

5. **Process.** To craft a card, perform the following steps:

   1. **Step 1: Choose Gear.** Choose an available Gear card you would like to craft (“available” means there are uncrafted copies).

   2. **Step 2: Spen Resources.** Lose resources equal to the cost found on the corresponding Technology card, as well as any other cards that are part of the cost.

   3. **Step 3: Add to Armory.** Add the crafted Gear card to the Argo Armory.

name: Titan Crafting.
rule:
Certain Technologies allow you to craft Titans. There are two types: Breeding and Fusing

1. **Breeding Technology.** Each Breeding Technology shows the costs of breeding a specific Titan and how many days it is going to take, if any.

   1. **Breeding.** When you use a Technology ability to breed a Titan, you pay any associated costs (usually a Dreamwalker Titan), then write the Titan’s class on the Timeline the indicated number of days from the current day. On that day, during the Advancement Step, you resolve it and gain the noted Titan.

   2. **One at a Time.** A given Breeding Technology ability cannot be used while an Event added by it is unresolved on the Timeline.

   3. **No Free Stockpiling.** A given Breeding Technology card cannot be used to place an Event on the Timeline with the same name as a Titan class you already have in your Titan Stoa.

2. **Fusing Technology.** Some Production Facilities allow you to craft Titans. Crafting Titans this way is called Fusing.

   1. **Fusing.** Fusing a Titan requires two things: 1 Gaia-born Titan (Dreamwalker) and 1 specific Primordial Core. When you pay this cost, immediately gain the new Titan.

3. **Prototype Titan Crafting.** If a Titan crafting Technology card describes a Titan class as a “Prototype”, that crafting recipe only works in the Cycle that that Technology card came from.

name: Timeline Events.
rule: If there are any Story events written on the Timeline, resolve them during this step.

name: Resolving Main Story.
rule:
If you meet your current Story card’s ([II.4.2](#bookmark=id.qj86klnr4d6k)) requirements, resolve the Story card effects in this step.

1. **Discard Tokens.** When you resolve the current story progression, discard all tokens collected on the current Story card (unless stated otherwise).

name: Clue Cards.
rule:
Each Cycle has a number of Clue cards that influence the game, introducing new rules, mechanics and hints. Clue cards might represent story passages, maps, layouts, events, and more.

   1. **Identifying Clue Cards.** Clue cards have a distinct back, showing the number of the Cycle they belong to and the number of the Story (or Doom) card they will be introduced with.

   2. **Secret.** You should never look at any Clue cards if you were not specifically instructed to.

3. **Using Clue Cards.** Other game elements will tell you how to use particular Clue cards.

name: Story Events.
rule: If there is a Story Event noted on your current day (i.e. an Event that is neither an Advancement nor a Battle) find the passage it leads to in the Storybook and read it.

name: Secret Codes.
rule:
If you find a secret 4-digit number on a game element, during the Story Step you may find the corresponding paragraph in the main story section of your current Storybook and resolve it.

1. **One-Time Use.** Unless stated otherwise, each secret code paragraph can only be resolved once.

name: Divine Surges.
rule:
Some Story Cards will instruct you to perform a Divine Surge. A Divine Surge allows you to replenish available Godforms ([X.7.19](#bookmark=id.dedfkul00tpi)), as well as Summonings and Summon Charges ([X.7.20](#bookmark=id.h9t3z22r85xe)).

   1. **Story-Driven.** Divine Surges are mainly associated with major breakthroughs in the Story.

2. **Performing.** Whenever you are instructed to perform a Divine Surge, simply erase all marks and names from the Godforms section of the Argo Sheet. Do the same for Summonings, then reset Summon Charges to your current Limit ([X.7.20.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.c6o4rsc6jxv)).

name: Resolving Doom.
rule:
If you meet your current Doom card’s ([II.4.4](#bookmark=id.agq6do4ukuc)) requirements, resolve the Doom card effects in this step.

   1. **Discard Tokens.** When you resolve the current doom progression, discard exactly as many tokens collected on the current Doom card as the requirement, and move all excess tokens to either the other side of the card (if progressing from side A) or the next Doom card (if progressing from side B).

2. **The End.** If you have to resolve Doom progression on Doom card 3B, you lose the game.

name: Tile Becomes Explored.
rule: During the End Step, if you are on an Unexplored tile, remove the token representing this and the tile becomes Explored.

name: Move Last Visited City.
rule: If you are on a Map tile with the City symbol, move the Last Visited City token to this tile ([VIII.6.1](#bookmark=id.iq5vmecxpzyn)).

name: Next Day.
rule: After you resolve the End Step, proceed to the next Campaign Round.

name: Timing.
rule:
At the end of any Campaign Round Step, you may decide to save your game progress and end your current game session.

1. **A Recommendation.** We advise saving at the end of the End Step so that you can begin your next game at the start of a new day.

name: Process.
rule:
To save your game, take out the Argo Sheet and perform the following steps:

   1. **Step 1: Note Story Card.** Note the number and letter of your current Story card along with the number of tokens on it (in parentheses).

2. **Step 2: Note Inward Odyssey.** If you have a single token on the Inward Odyssey card, note that.

   3. **Step 3: Note Doom Card.** Note the number and letter of your current Doom card along with the number of tokens on it (in parentheses).

4. **Step 4: Note Current Map Tile.** Note the number of the current Map tile the Argo is on.

   5. **Step 5: Note Other Map Tiles.** On the last page of the sheet, to the right of the Choice Matrix, note any other Map tile numbers relevant to the current Story and Doom cards, as well as all numbers of Map tiles with other game elements, such as tokens, tiles, and the Adversary.

6. **Step 6: Note Triskelions.** Note the current Triskelion stats of each Argonaut on their respective Argonaut Sheets.

   7. **Step 7: Store Unlocked Cards.** Store your Technology deck, Projects deck, Argo Armory deck, Exploration deck, Mnemos Vault, gained Clue cards and all unlocked Godform and Summoning cards separate from other components of the same type, to easily access them when you resume your campaign.

8. **Step 8: Return Map Tiles.** Place all Explored Map tiles into the Explored Map space in the game insert.

   1. **Future Sessions.** During the next game session, you may reconstruct the whole Map at the start or only reconstruct the parts relevant for that game session. If you don’t reconstruct the whole Map, be sure to leave all the unused Explored Map tiles in the Explored Map space in the game insert.

name: Adventures.
rule: Everything that sends you to a Storybook paragraph is considered an adventure.

name: Storybook Golden Rule.
rule: Never read story passages unless you are clearly instructed to and only read the paragraphs you have been directed to.

name: Following the Paragraphs.
rule:
1. **Chaining Paragraphs.** Many Storybook paragraphs include an outcome pointing you to another paragraph. When you are done resolving a paragraph along with all of its associated effects, simply proceed to the paragraph you were directed to.

   2. **End of Adventure.** If a story paragraph states “Return to the Voyage Phase” or simply concludes with no further direction, the adventure is over. Return to the Campaign Round Step you were on when you were initially instructed to read the paragraph.

3. **Must Fully Resolve.** The adventure ends only if you are instructed to “Return to the Voyage Phase” or there is no further paragraph mentioned. You must always resolve all chained paragraphs before you can perform any other actions or resolve further Campaign Round Steps.

name: Story Choices.
rule:
Some of the story paragraphs have choice-based outcomes. Depending on your decision, they will point you to different paragraphs, and sometimes will list some of the gains and losses associated with a given choice.

1. **Peril:** A story choice, test, or roll with “Peril” written in it must be made exclusively by the party leader. Do not consult other players.

2. **Conditional.** Some choices are only available if you fulfill specific conditions. If you do not meet the conditions, you cannot choose that story choice.

   3. **Rarely Forced.** You are rarely forced to choose a given option. Do so only when explicitly told to.

4. **Taking Notes.** Some story paragraphs will instruct you to note some information. If they specify where to note it, write it down in the appropriate section of your Argo Sheet or Argonaut Sheet. Otherwise, simply remember it for the duration of your current adventure.

name: Choice Matrix.
rule:
The Choice Matrix is a system used to track important choices and refer to them during future events.

   1. **Marking a Node.** Whenever the game instructs you to mark a Choice Matrix node, you should take your Argo Sheet and mark an appropriate space. You cannot mark a box twice. If a box is already marked when the game instructs you to mark it, ignore this instruction.

   2. **Node Triggers.** Nodes are used as Triggers for a variety of effects, events, and, most frequently, story choices.

3. **Crossing Out a Node.** When instructed to “cross out” or “erase” a Choice Matrix node, erase it or encircle it, to note that the choice has been reversed.

4. **Persistent.** The Choice Matrix persists through ALL the Cycles of Aeon Trespass: Odyssey. When you progress from one Cycle to the next, carry over all marked choices to the Choice Matrix of your new Argo Sheet (or simply use the same last page).

name: Party Leader.
rule:
1. **Choosing the Party Leader.** During each adventure, one of the Argonauts must become the party leader. Unless instructed otherwise, choose a party leader as soon as they are referred to in the paragraph (either in the story or in a mechanical instruction) or when you are faced with a choice (whichever comes first). Once the party leader is chosen, give them the Priority Target token as a reminder. This player remains the party leader until the end of this adventure.

   1. **Choosing Randomly.** If you cannot reach an agreement on who should be the party leader, choose one randomly.

   2. **Tiebreaker.** Most importantly, the party leader breaks all decision ties during the adventure if the players cannot reach a compromise.

3. **Consequence Receiver.** They are also the most likely to be tested during the adventure, as well as to gain Triskelion statistics (Danger, Fate, or Rage), Boons, Afflictions, tokens, and various other game elements.

name: Adventure Tests and Rolls.
rule:
1. **Adventure Tests.** Many adventures will instruct you to perform a test. To do so, roll a d10 and compare the result to the difficulty of the test. If the result is equal to or higher than the difficulty, it is a Success. Otherwise it is a Fail.

   1. **Bonuses.** A variety of things will give you a bonus to adventure tests. Unless stated otherwise, all of these effects must be declared before you perform the test. Then, after you roll the die, increase the result by the bonus.

   2. **Argonaut Tests.** Tests performed by a specific Argonaut (or multiple Argonauts) associated with a specific Argonaut Skill. You must increase the test result by your Skill value. There are two types of Argonaut tests in the Adventure Phase:

   1. **Solo Tests.** Some tests will require only one Argonaut to make the test (usually the party leader), while others will have each Argonaut make a separate test. A solo test is a make it or break it affair, the Argonaut will either succeed or fail. Sometimes, other Argonauts will get the chance to influence the test.

   2. **Group Tests.** Group tests have success ranges. After every Argonaut makes their test, the Argonauts sum up their successes and consult the results. This means a group test usually has more than two possible outcomes.

      1. **Fail Effects.** Group tests also introduce “Fail:” effects. A Fail effect is applied to the individual Argonaut who failed the test and not the whole group. *(it is possible for an individual Argonaut to fail but a group test to still succeed.)*

3. **Other Adventure Rolls.** There are many types of rolls you may be required to make during an adventure. Not all rolls are tests. An effect may instruct you to simply “roll a d10” and resolve it according to the result. If it does not specifically include the word ‘test’, it cannot be re-rolled using Fate.

name: Fated Boxes.
rule:
Fated boxes are commonly found in adventure tables. They are used to denote that you have already encountered this part of the story.

   1. **Marking.** Mark a fated box in an adventure table immediately upon being instructed to.

2. **Rolling an Adventure Again.** If you have already marked one fated box corresponding to an adventure and rolled its number again, check the following in order and resolve the first one that matches (only).

   1. **Check 1: Check for Another Box.** Check if there is another fated box associated with the adventure. If there is, simply mark it and follow the instructions denoted next to it.

   2. **Check 2: Reroll.** If all fated boxes associated with a given adventure are marked, roll a d10 again and resolve a different adventure instead.

   3. **All Marked.** If at any point the fated boxes of all adventures in any of the adventure tables have been marked, reset (erase) all of the boxes in that table.

   4. **Outside of Adventure Tables.** Fated boxes may also be encountered in some of the paragraphs, outside of the adventure tables. These paragraphs will inform you when to mark the fated boxes and may introduce additional effects referring to the number of marked fated boxes.

5. **Persistent Between Playthroughs.** Fated boxes carry over between all your playthroughs of Aeon Trespass: Odyssey. If you repeat any of the Cycles, do not reset marked fated boxes.

name: Story Adventures.
rule:
Story Adventures are all passages in the Main Story section of the book. Usually, you will be directed to them by Story ([II.4.2](#bookmark=id.qj86klnr4d6k)) and Doom cards ([II.4.4](#bookmark=id.agq6do4ukuc)), but there are some other, unique effects that may lead to Story Adventures as well.

1. **Default.** If a game element directs you to a passage without telling you which section you can find it with, you should refer to the Main Story section.

name: Hub Adventures.
rule:
Besides the main story, most of the adventures will come from your Cycle’s Adventure Hubs. You resolve a Hub Adventure each time you encounter an unexplored adventure symbol. When you do so, resolve the following steps in order.

1. **Step 1: Determine Hub.** To find out which adventure you should resolve, find the Adventure Hubs table on the first page of your current Storybook. Roll a d10 and refer to the column corresponding to your current Story card. This will inform you which Adventure Hub you should go to as well as which Adventure Terrain you should add to the next Battle. If you have already completed the rolled Adventure Hub (its Omega box is marked on the Argo sheet), reroll the d10 until you roll an Adventure Hub you have not completed.

   2. **Step 2: Note Adventure Terrain.** Once you establish which Adventure Hub you are about to resolve, consult the Adventure Terrain column in the same row. Note this Terrain and its placement instructions on the Timeline, on the same day as the next Timeline Battle.

   1. **Timeline Only.** This Terrain affects only a Timeline Battle, not a Battle triggered in any other way.

   2. **Limited to 1\.** If an Adventure Terrain is already noted for the next Timeline Battle day, ignore this step.

   3. **Step 3: Determine Adventure.** Once you know which Hub you have encountered, mark a box on its adventure track on the Argo Sheet. Then go to the corresponding chapter in the Storybook. There, you will find an Adventure Table, listing all adventures available in a given Hub.

   1. **Premarked Box.** If the box you marked on the Argo Sheet has an Alpha or Omega symbol, simply proceed to resolve the paragraph marked with the same symbol in this Hub’s chapter.

2. **Empty Box.** If the box you marked was empty, instead roll a d10 again and consult the Hub’s Adventure Table to find the adventure you have to resolve. Mark a fated box ([IX.1.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.rddwmukbup3z)) next to this adventure’s name, then find it in the Hub’s chapter. If you have already completed the rolled Adventure Hub (its Fated box is marked in the Storybook), reroll the d10 until you roll an Adventure you have not completed.

4. **Step 4: Gain Mnemos Nodes.** Once you find the correct paragraph, gain Mnemos nodes, as follows:

   1. **Step 1: Find Adventure Trait.** Look at the Trait associated with this particular adventure, which is noted at the top of its opening paragraph.

   2. **Step 2: Check Cards.** Each Argonaut checks each of their Mnemos and Fated Mnemos cards they possess in descending order of most to least Mnemos Nodes. For each card, gain 1 Mnemos Node for that card if that card has a matching trait, only if you haven’t gained a Mnemos Node for this card type (Mnemos or Fated Mnemos) yet this adventure.

5. **Step 5: Begin the Adventure.** Finally, read the paragraph and see where the adventure takes you.

name: R\&R Adventures.
rule: 1. **Resolving an R\&R Symbol.** When you encounter an R\&R adventure symbol, during the Expedition Step go to the R\&R adventure chapter of your current Storybook. Roll a d10 and consult the R\&R Adventure Table to find the adventure you have to resolve. Mark a fated box ([IX.1.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.rddwmukbup3z)) next to this adventure’s name, then find it in the R\&R Adventures chapter and read it.

name: Inward Odyssey.
rule:
1. **Inward Odyssey Card.** Each Cycle has its own Inward Odyssey Story card. That Story card serves as a table of contents for Inward Odyssey adventures as well as a place to put Progress.

2. **Gaining Argo Knowledge.** Argo Knowledge is a semi-frequent reward from adventures, but you can also place Progress tokens on the Inward Odyssey card to gain 1 Argo Knowledge for every 2 tokens.

   3. **Triggering Inward Odysseys.** Each time you gain Argo Knowledge, trigger the next Inward Odyssey adventure.

   4. **Timing.** You will resolve this adventure at the end of your current Step. This means that Inward Odyssey adventures have the potential to trigger during any Step of the Round. If you ever gain more than 1 during a given Step, you will resolve all of the corresponding Inward Odyssey adventures, one by one.

   5. **Progressing Cycles.** Once you clear your current Cycle and move to the next one, you will change Inward Odyssey cards. Any Inward Odyssey stories you haven’t experienced by that point can no longer be resolved in that playthrough.

6. **Inward Odyssey Adventure Requirements.** Some Inward Odyssey adventures may have specific requirements, like having a certain Technology or advancing past a certain point in the game. When you encounter a requirement you do not yet meet, stop resolving that adventure. Later, when you meet the requirements, resolve that adventure at the end of the current Step (you do not need to gain additional Argo Knowledge to do so). If you have an unresolved Inward Odyssey adventure and gain another Argo Knowledge, move to the next adventure on the card. Note any omitted adventures on the Argo Sheet.

name: Mnemos Breakthrough Adventures.
rule:
1. **Trigger.** Mnemos Breakthroughs are adventures triggered by an Argonaut’s Mnemos cards ([III.5.2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.kivud9c0csee)).

2. **Resolving.** During the Story Step, if all nodes to the left of a breakthrough symbol have been marked ([III.5.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.guu3djjyeg00)), and the breakthrough symbol itself is unmarked, resolve the associated paragraph (noted above this breakthrough symbol), then mark the breakthrough symbol.

3. **New Mnemos Ability.** When you resolve a Mnemos Breakthrough, you unlock the next Mnemos ability ([III.5.2.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.fcu0r7i6xbaj)).

4. **Other Rewards.** Note any additional rewards on your Argonaut Sheet.

5. **Rewards from Other Cycles.** If you gain a Gear card from a previous Cycle as a Mnemos Breakthrough reward, Ascend it to your current Cycle.

name: Dreams of Pharos.
rule:
1. **Triggers.** Dreams of Pharos are mysterious adventures triggered by specific game effects and usually added to the Timeline as Story Events.

2. **Resolving.** To resolve a Dreams of Pharos, go to the Dreams of Pharos chapter of your current Storybook. Roll a d10 and consult the Dreams of Pharos Adventure Table to find the adventure you have to resolve. Mark a fated box ([IX.1.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.rddwmukbup3z)) next to this adventure’s name, then find it in the Dreams of Pharos chapter and read it.

3. **Rewards.** The main reward for resolving a Dream of Pharos is cryptic knowledge\! it is for you to figure it out.

   4. **Pharos Delve.** Most Dreams of Pharos include a push your luck mechanic called a Pharos Delve.

   1. **Resolving.** When you see the Pharos Delve prompt, start drawing cards from the Major Trauma deck, adding the numbers in the lower left corner of the cards. After each draw, you may decide to either stop the Delve or to continue. If you continue, draw another card and add the number denoted on it.

   2. **Delve Difficulty.** The Delve Difficulty is a number denoted in parentheses next to the success effect in the prompt.

   3. **Succeeding.** To succeed in a Delve, the sum of your cards must be equal to or higher than the Delve difficulty, but lower than 16\. When you choose to stop drawing and both of the above conditions are true, resolve the Success effect.

   4. **Failing.** If the total is at any point equal to or higher than 16, you immediately Fail the Pharos Delve and resolve the Fail effect.

   5. **Backing Out.** If you decide to stop the Delve before reaching the Delve Difficulty, nothing happens.

   6. **In Other Elements.** Some other game effects may also use the Pharos Delve mechanic. When an effect tells you to use the Pharos Delve rules, it means it will be resolved exactly like a Pharos Delve, but it is not considered a Pharos Delve or a Dream of Pharos.

   7. **Standard Deck Composition.** There are 15 cards in a standard Major Trauma deck. There is a pair each of 1’s, 2's, 3’s, 4’s, 5’s, 6’s, and 7’s, and then a single 8\.

name: Special Events.
rule:
Special Events are a broad category for unique, usually short and one of a kind adventures.

1. **Resolving.** When instructed to resolve a Special Event, simply find an appropriately named passage in the Special Events chapter of your current Storybook and read it.

name: Overview
rule: . In Battle, each player controls an Argonaut/Titan pair, while the Primordial is controlled by a deck of advanced AI cards. The Titans (usually four of them) want to deal a set number of Wounds to slay the Primordial, while the Primordial tries to kill the Titans. Whoever prevails, wins.

name: Titan.
rule: A Titan is represented by a Titan Sheet and a Titan miniature. In some cases, this term will also include the Argonaut junctioning with the Titan.

name: Primordial.
rule: The Primordial is represented by the Primordial Sheet and a Primordial Miniature.

name: Battle Board.
rule:
The Battle Board is made up of spaces. Only spaces and their edges are considered to make up the Battle Board.

1. **Short and Long Edges.** The Battle Board has two short and two long Board Edges. Short Board Edges are marked with letters to identify rows, and the long Board Edges are marked with numbers to identify columns.

name: Spaces.
rule:
Spaces are square boxes you place other game elements on. A Titan takes up one space. A Primordial typically takes up four or nine spaces.

   1. **Occupied.** Spaces are occupied if there is a miniature or an Obstacle Terrain tile on them.

2. **Empty.** All unoccupied spaces are considered empty spaces.

name: Adjacent.
rule: Something is adjacent if the space it is on shares at least one edge with the other space.

name: Board Edge.
rule: Edges not shared with another space are considered to be part of the Board Edge.

name: Attack Dice.
rule: Attack Dice are d10s ([I.4.1](#bookmark=id.evuvd9dd6v2c)) used during the Attack Roll step of a Titan Attack.

name: Evasion Dice.
rule: Evasion Dice are d10s ([I.4.1](#bookmark=id.evuvd9dd6v2c)) used during the Evasion Roll step of a Primordial Attack Action.

name: Kratos Tokens.
rule:
Kratos tokens are tokens which are placed into the Kratos Pool as a result of weapon abilities, Kratos Tables, or other game effects, and are used exclusively during Titan Attacks.

1. **Breaks.** Attacking Titans may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use any number of Break tokens during the Power Roll step to turn 1 Potential symbol into 1 Power for each token used.

2. **Openings.** Attacking Titans may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use any number of Opening tokens during the Attack Roll step to gain 1 Precision for each token used.

3. **Diversion.** If there is a Diversion token in the Kratos Pool, the attacking Titan may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use it to ignore Fail Responses.

4. **Fire.** During the Power Roll step of a Titan Attack, the Attacking Titan must turn 1 Potential symbol into 1 Power for each Fire token in the pool *(other tokens may be used first)*. Remove used Fire tokens after the Power Roll step. Then, during the Clear the Kratos Pool step, remove only 1 Fire token and leave the rest in the Kratos Pool.

5. **Rouse.** For each Rouse token in the Kratos Pool, the attacking Titan may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) treat their Rage as if it were 1 higher. *(This does not cause you to die if you have to treat your Rage as higher than 9.)*

6. **Black.** Attacking Titans may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use any number of Black tokens during the Power Roll step to reroll a Power die and then may convert 1 Potential symbol on that die into 1 Power for each token used.

7. **Hope.** Attacking Titans may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use any number of Hope tokens during the Power Roll step to turn 1 Potential symbol or 1 Dot symbol into 1 Power for each token used.

8. **Closing.** Attacking Titans may[2](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yo3y8htv7ww) use any number of  Closing tokens to gain 1 Precision for each token used and then may re-roll 1 of their Attack dice with no additional cost for each token used.

name: Kratos Pool.
rule: The area where you place Kratos tokens is called the Kratos Pool.

name: Line of Sight.
rule:
Line of Sight (LoS) is used to determine whether the Primordial has an unobstructed view of a Titan and vice versa. A Titan must have LoS to the Primordial to perform an Attack. The Primordial must have LoS to the Target if the command line requires it to be in front or in sight. Targeting instructions will explicitly say if they require LoS.

1. **Checking LoS.** To check Line of Sight, draw an imaginary straight line from a corner of any space occupied by the Attacker to a corner of any space occupied by the Target. The Attacker has Line of Sight if any such line passes through no Obscuring ([X.8.9.11](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.dppjtz3ibjm9)) Terrain tiles (along edges counts as going through, but through corners does not).

   1. **Standing on Obscuring Terrain.** Obscuring terrain does not block LoS to miniatures standing on it.

   2. **Blindspots and VPs.** Blindspots and Vantage Points are never in a Primordial’s Line of Sight.

   3. **Miniatures.** A miniature never blocks Line of Sight.

name: Priority Target Token.
rule:
The Priority Target token is used to denote who the current Priority Target is ([VI.7.2.2](#bookmark=id.tzgrihsrjame)).

   1. **Gaining Priority.** When you increase your Rage for any reason, compare your current Rage to the Rage of the Priority Target token’s holder. If your Rage is higher, you become the new Priority Target and gain the token.

   2. **Start of Battle.** At the start of the Battle assign the token to a Titan with the highest Rage. If two or more Titans are tied, choose which one (out of the tied Titans) to assign the Priority Target token to.

   3. **Dying While Priority Target.** When the Titan with the Priority Target token dies, pass the token to the living Titan with the highest Rage.

   4. **Gaining from Other Effects.** Some effects state that a Titan gains and then cannot lose the Priority Target token. If there are multiple such effects in play at the same time, the Titan that got it the earliest remains the Priority Target. For the purpose of passing the token after the Titan’s death, Titans with such effects are treated as if their Rage was 9\.

name: Step 1: Battle Scenario.
rule: When instructed to resolve a Battle, unless directed to a specific page, find the Battle Scenario corresponding to the particular Primordial you are facing. You will find the scenarios of a Battle from a particular Cycle in that Cycle’s Storybook, in the last few pages. Be careful to only read the Scenarios you are instructed to.

name: Step 2: Perform Setup.
rule:
To set up a Battle, perform the following in order.

1. **Step 1: Battle Board.** Place the Battle Board on the table.

2. **Step 2: Decks, Dice, and Tokens.** Shuffle the individual Trauma decks, the Kratos deck and the Moiros deck. Place all decks in the designated spaces (indicated on the edges of the Battle Board) and place Kratos tokens, Attack dice, and Power dice within reach of all players.

3. **Step 3: Argonauts.** Place each Argonaut Portrait with its associated Mnemos card(s) in front of whoever is controlling that Argonaut.

4. **Step 4: Triskelions.** Place each associated Triskelion near their other components. The Triskelion stats do not reset at the beginning of Battle; you start with the Triskelion stats you have accumulated over the course of gameplay since the last Battle.

5. **Step 5: Titans.** Choose the Titan each Argonaut will control from the available Titans and take its Titan Sheet and miniature, as well as any patterns it may have. If at least four Titans are available, each Argonaut must control one Titan.

   6. **Step 6: Gear Loadouts.** Players as a group assign Gear cards from the Argo Armory to the Titans. Each Titan can only have one copy of the same Support card.

   1. **Charges.** Place a number of Charge tokens on Gear cards with Charge costs equal to the shown value.

   2. **Transforming.** Players may choose which side of each Gear card with the Transform keyword should start face up.

7. **Step 7: Argo Abilities.** Players choose the Argo Abilities they want to use during the upcoming Battle. You may use as many Argo Ability cards as your Argo Ability Limit ([X.4.11.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.scbni846db32)) allows. Place the Argo Abilities you have chosen next to the Battle Board, along with the indicated number of Charge tokens.

   8. **Step 8: Primordial Setup.** Place the Primordial Sheet of the Primordial you are about to fight in the designated space near the Battle Board.

   1. **Signature and Routine.** Place its Routine and Signature cards in the corresponding spaces *(be careful not to use the Mnestis Routine side, unless you are currently playing a Mnestis Theatre Battle)*.

   2. **AI Cards.** Take all of the Primordial’s AI cards and divide them into three decks based on the roman numeral on the back of the cards. Place the deck numbered “I” and place it on the appropriate space at the bottom of the Battle Board, this is the AI deck. Place decks numbered “II” and “III” directly below, these are the Escalation decks. If there is an AI 0 card, place it on top of the AI deck.

   3. **BP Cards.** Repeat *b*, but for BP cards.

   4. **Trait Cards.** Find the Trait cards for any Traits that are not described on the Primordial Sheet and place them nearby as a reference.

   9. **Step 9: Terrain Placement.** Find all the Terrain cards and Terrain tiles used in the Battle as listed in the Scenario. Place the Terrain cards near the Battle Board and the Terrain tiles on the Battle Board according to the Battle Diagram.

   10. **Step 10: Adventure Terrain.** Check the current day on the Timeline for any marked Adventure Terrain. If there is any marked, place the corresponding Terrain tile(s) on the Battle Board, after you place all Battle-specific Terrain tiles. This Terrain tile(s) cannot be placed in a way that would overlap with Titan starting positions, the Primordial, or other Terrain tiles.

   1. **Outer 3\.** Tile must be placed only on spaces within 3 spaces of any Board Edge.

   2. **Inner 3\.** Tile must be placed only on spaces not within 3 spaces of any Board Edge.

   3. **Other.** Individual Adventure Terrain cards may have specific placement rules.

   11. **Step 11: Titan Placement.** Place Titans on Titan Starting Positions as indicated by the Battle Diagram. If you have fewer than four Titans on the Argo, you must start the Battle with as many Titans as possible. Every Argonaut that is not assigned a Titan will not participate in Battle.

   1. **Starting a Battle with Zero Titans.** If you are ever forced to do Battle without any Titans onboard, you immediately lose the campaign\!

12. **Step 12: Primordial Placement.** Place the Primordial as instructed by the Scenario.

name: Step 3: Resolve the Battle.
rule:
To Resolve a Battle, resolve alternating Primordial and Titan Rounds. After completing either Round, proceed to the other one. Repeat this process until a Victory or Defeat condition has been met.

   1. **Begin with the Primordial Round.** Unless an effect explicitly states otherwise, all Battles begin with the Primordial Round.

   2. **Victory.** The Battle immediately ends with the Argonauts’ Victory as soon as the Primordial has suffered Wounds equal to its Wounds value, or another Victory condition listed in the Scenario is met.

3. **Defeat.** The Battle immediately ends with the Argonauts’ Defeat as soon as all Titans die, the Argonauts decide to Retreat, or another Defeat condition listed in the Scenario is met.

name: Step 4: Resolve the Aftermath.
rule: After the Battle is over, resolve the Battle Aftermath ([X.6](#bookmark=id.y4z2hocmgxeo))

name: Start of the Primordial Round.
rule: If the Primordial has an effect that triggers at the start of the Primordial Round, do so before anything else in the Primordial Round happens.

name: Resolve AI.
rule:
Draw and resolve an AI card ([VI.6.3](#bookmark=id.eqfvgaa6vvk2)).

1. **Clean Up.** Once you finish resolving the AI card, discard it. Then, if there are no more cards in the AI deck, immediately shuffle the discard to form the new AI deck.

name: End of Primordial Round.
rule: If the Primordial has an effect that triggers at the end of the Primordial Round, do so after resolving all other effects in the Primordial Round.

name: Start of Titan Round.
rule: Sometimes a game effect will trigger at the start of the Titan Round. In that case, it is resolved before any of the Titans take their turns.

name: Titan Turns.
rule:
During the Titan Round, each Titan takes one turn. Once a Titan has taken a turn, it cannot do so again this Round.

1. **Turn Order.** Titan turns may be taken in any order.

   2. **Must Be Fully Resolved.** You must completely resolve one Titan turn before you start another. You cannot interrupt one Titan’s turn to take another Titan’s turn.

3. **Actions.** At the start of its turn, a Titan gains one Combat Action and one Movement Action, and they may use unlimited Free Actions. Actions have no set order, except that they cannot be taken in the middle of another action, and Free Actions can only be used while at least one of a Titan’s Combat Action or Movement Action is unspent.

4. **Actions Are Not Forced.** You are not forced to spend all or any of a Titan’s actions, but unused actions are lost when the Titan’s turn ends.

5. **Gaining Extra Actions.** If a Titan gains an extra action during their turn, that extra action must be spent immediately or be forfeited.

6. **Ends Immediately.** A Titan’s turn ends immediately once it has spent all of its available Combat Actions and Movement Actions (or has chosen to forgo any remaining). When this happens, turn the Argonaut card 90 degrees to the right to mark that Argonaut’s Titan as already having taken its turn this round *(this is not under any circumstances considered to be exhausting a card)*. Once all Titans have taken their turns, turn all Argonaut cards back to their normal positions.

name: Titan Movement.
rule:
1. **Movement Action.** As a Free Action, you can spend a Titan’s Movement Action to move that Titan a number of spaces up to its Speed value. This active effect is not restricted to once per round.

   2. **Move-like Ability.** Move-like abilities are any voluntary effect that changes a Titan’s position on the board that isn’t a Movement Action. Move-like abilities are not considered a Movement Action for the purpose of other abilities and effects.

   3. **Titan Movement Rules.**

1. **Resolving Terrain Effects.** Whenever a Titan moves for any reason, always move it one space at a time, resolving the effects of each space of Terrain tile it moves through or ends the movement on. A Titan cannot move through an Obstacle  ([X.8.9.12](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.st2kkdez2scx)).

2. **Voluntary Movement.** When a Titan is performing voluntary movement of any kind, it cannot move through Chasms ([X.8.9.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.4orh8epluegr)), other Titans, or the Primordial. It also cannot move onto a space it was previously on during this movement.

   3. **Involuntary Movement.** When a Titan is forced to move Involuntarily, you must resolve that Involuntary Movement up to its maximum value unless another effect forces you to stop.

4. **Ending on Another Titan.** If a Titan ends involuntary movement on a space occupied by another Titan, the other Titan is displaced ([A.28](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.tg7nazle64h0)).

   5. **Moving Diagonally.** Some Titans can move diagonally. When a Titan moves diagonally, it cannot move into a space if doing so would cause it to cross over a corner of an occupied space.

   6. **Teleport.** When an effect instructs you to teleport a Titan, place it on the indicated space. If no space was indicated, place it adjacent to the Primordial, in front if possible.

name: Titan Attacks.
rule:
When a Titan is instructed to resolve an Attack, resolve a Titan Attack Sequence ([X.5](#bookmark=id.e8h6h6ly3clq)).

1. **Combat Action.** As a Free Action, you can spend a Titan’s Combat Action to perform a Titan Attack. This active effect is not restricted to once per round.

name: Unexhausting.
rule: A Titan’s Movement Action or Combat Action may be spent to unexhaust all exhausted equipped game elements (cards, Mnemos cards, Titan abilities, and so on).

name: Free Actions.
rule:
A Free Action is required to use an active ability on a Gear card, Mnemos card, or Titan sheet. During a Titan’s turn, it can perform any number of free actions, but each of them can only be performed once that turn.

1. **Timing.** Free actions can only be performed before a Titan’s Movement or Combat action and only during that Titan’s Turn.

name: Argo Abilities.
rule:
Argo Abilities are Technologies that can be used during a Battle.

   1. **Argo Ability Charges.** Each Argo Ability has a number of charges it gains at the start of Battle.

2. **Using Argo Abilities.** Argo Abilities can be activated any time a Free Action could be, before any Titan turn, or as specified on the card. All non-trireme Argo Abilities have a cost of Exhaust and 1 Energy. Trireme Argo Abilities have any other costs specified.

   3. **Unexhausting.** Argo Abilities unexhaust (for free) at the end of each Round (both Primordial and Titan). This is the only way they can unexhaust..

4. **Argo Ability Limit.** The current Propylon Technology dictates how many Argo Abilities you have available during a single Battle. This is only relevant during Battle setup ([X.2.2.7](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.7iqe7fvwgc8n)).

name: Trireme Argo Abilities.
rule:
Trireme Argo Abilities are Argo Abilities, but you cannot have more than one Trireme Argo Ability prepared at the same time.

1. **Preparing.** When you prepare an Argo Ability with the *Trireme* keyword, place the Trireme token on that Argo Ability, blue side up.

   2. **Moving the Trireme.** Once per Titan Round, you may move the Trireme...

   1. …(Cycle 2\) to any River Works tile if it is on the card.

   2. …(Cycle 2\) to any River Works tile space connected to its current space by River Works tiles.

   3. …(Cycle 4\) onto an empty space on the Battle Board, following any restrictions on the specific card.

   4. …from any space to the card, only if there is no Titan on the Trireme.

3. **Trireme Space.** The space (only) with the Trireme loses the Chasm keyword. A Titan can stand on this space, and moves with the Trireme if the Trireme moves.

4. **Primordial.** If the Primordial moves onto the Trireme, discard the Trireme token.

name: 13th Muse Grand Cannon.
rule:
During Cycle 2, you will unlock the 13th Muse Grand Cannon. Treat the 13th Muse Grand Cannon as an Argo Ability, except it does not count towards the Argo Ability Limit. Specific rules are listed on the 13th Muse Grand Cannon card.

1. **Preparing.** When you prepare the 13th Muse Grand Cannon, if you have any Payload cards, you may choose to place one on the Payload slot. That Payload then overrides all default payload options on the 13th Muse Grand Cannon.

name: End of Titan Round.
rule: Sometimes a game effect will trigger at the end of the Titan Round. In that case, it is resolved after all of the Titans take their turns.

name: Step 1: Declare Attack, Active Weapon, and Target.
rule:
Declare you are about to Attack, then choose one of your unexhausted Weapon cards as the Active Weapon for this Attack. You must also choose the Target for your Attack, which can be anything on the Battle Board with a To-Hit value.

1. **Cancel Chain Break.** If an Attack ends in this step, it does not cause a Chain Break ([X.7.6](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.yx8r43h369el)).

2. **Effective Range.** You can only choose a weapon as active if the target of your Attack is in its effective range and LoS ([X.1.11](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.uhlbsa6j3qmu)). If a weapon does not have a Ranged or Reach keyword, it is Melee. If you cannot choose a legal active weapon or don’t have LoS, you cannot perform an Attack. Do not resolve any further steps of the attack sequence.

name: Step 2: Raise Rage and Check Priority Target.
rule: Gain 1 Rage. Then, if your Rage is higher than the Priority Target’s Rage, gain the Priority Target token.

name: Step 3: Attack Roll.
rule:
In the upper left corner of the active weapon card, there is a number of Attack dice. Roll the specified number of dice.

1. **Rerolls.** If the Titan has any rerolls (or wants to reroll with Fate), they may be used before counting hits.

2. **Gaining a Crit Chance.** If you roll a natural ‘10’ with the Crit Die ([I.4.1.1](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.h2hv0pqoghti)) on an Attack Roll, gain a Crit Chance for this Attack.

name: Step 3.5: Count Hits.
rule:
Add any Precision bonus the Titan may have to each Attack die rolled, then deal hits for each die result that meets or beats the Primordial’s To-Hit value.

1. **Full Miss.** If there are zero hits, it is a Full Miss. Resolve the Clear the Kratos Pool Step ([X.5.11](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.z8uw7pfhu8ru)) immediately, then skip all remaining steps of this Attack.

name: Step 4: First Ability Window.
rule: The First Ability Window is exclusively a moment in time. Many Abilities have a timing condition for this moment.

name: Step 5: Draw BP Card.
rule: Draw the top card from the BP deck and reveal it.

name: Step 6: Power Roll.
rule:
1. **Step 1: Create the Power Pool.** Each successful hit grants you as many Power dice as your active weapon’s Weapon Power attribute ([V.2.1.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.cawgjk2ue56v)), plus any Bonus Power attribute ([V.2.1.4](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.jcbxqvh5r7jw)), plus the Titan’s Power die and any other bonuses from your Kratos Table, equipped non-weapon Gear cards, Mnemos cards, and other applicable game effects.

   1. **Upgrading Power Dice.** There are four tiers of Power dice, which are (in ascending order): Red, Black, White, and Mortal. If an effect instructs you to use a die of a higher tier, use a die one tier higher.

2. **Power Dice Limit.** If you are required to add a die to the Power Pool and there are no dice of that type available, roll a die one tier higher instead of two of these dice *(If you were required to roll 7 red Power dice, instead roll 5 red dice and 1 black die)*. If you ever, after applying the above rule, need to roll dice of which there are no more available, you simply do not get to roll them[10](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=kix.cpbzikkpbx61).

   2. **Step 2: Roll the Dice.** Take all the dice in your Power Pool and roll them.

   1. **Use Power Re-rolls.** You may use any Power Rerolls you have. *(Power dice cannot be re-rolled using Fate.)*

   3. **Step 3: Resolve The Power Roll.** Sum up all Power from Power dice, converted Potentials, and any other effects; This is the Total Power of this Attack.

   1. **Potentials.** Each rolled Potential symbol gives you one Potential. These do not provide you with any benefits themselves, but they can be turned into Power using various effects.

name: Step 6.5: Wounds.
rule:
1. **Successfully Wounding.** If your Total Power is equal to or higher than the BP card’s AT value ([VI.11.1.5](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.l0ejzgpgno2b)), you deal a Wound. If you have a Crit Chance, you deal a Critical Wound instead.

2. **Failing to Wound.** If your Total Power is less than the AT value, you Fail to wound. Place the BP card you targeted on top of the BP discard pile.

3. **Wound Stack.** The Wound Stack is a stack of all Wound cards dealt in this Battle.

   1. **Single Wounds.** All Single Wound cards, as well BP I and BP II cards, in the Wound Stack count as one Wound dealt.

   2. **Double Wounds.** All Double Wound cards, as well BP III cards, in the Wound Stack count as two Wounds dealt.

   4. **Dealing Wounds.**

   1. **Wounding a BP I or BP II.** If you wound a BP I or BP II card, place it faceup on top of the Wound Stack.

2. **Wounding a BP III.** If you wound a BP III card, place a generic Double Wound card on top of the Wound Stack and set the wounded BP III card aside. It will return during Escalation ([X.5.10.9](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.ife7ovbw6c01)).

   3. **Critically Wounding a BP III.** If you Critically Wound a BP III card, place it on top of the Wound Stack.

   4. **Dealing Additional Wounds.** If an effect instructs you to deal an additional Wound, place a respective generic Wound card on top of the Wound Stack, in addition to any other Wounds you have dealt.

5. **Victory.** After placing a Wound in the Wound Stack, if the number of Wounds in the Wound Stack is at least the Primordial’s Wounds value ([VI.4.3](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1u3q2ngOWhVvixLbz3PiR-4HG0A1OpcAzCM-9cveigu4/edit?tab=t.0#bookmark=id.805jqbas3e3r)), the Primordial is slain and the Battle immediately ends with the Argonauts’ victory and you proceed to the Aftermath.
